user_input:
Dear Henry,
I hope this email finds you well!
I would like to load lammps gpu in my account, but I got the error of "sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account or account/partition combination specified". My slurm script is attached. Could you please let me know the correct format of the slurm script?
Many thanks!
Best Regards,
Ruitian
-------------------------------------------------
Dr. Ruitian He
Postdoctoral Research Assistant
Oxford Thermofluids Institute
Department of Engineering Science
University of Oxford
Oxford, OX2 0ES
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Dawn / Slurm Account / Project Charging
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (job submission blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is trying to submit a Dawn GPU job and receives:

  sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account or account/partition combination specified

On Dawn, this error usually means that the `#SBATCH -A` line is using the wrong Slurm charging account or that the account/partition combination is invalid. The safe guidance is to tell the user to use their project charging account rather than their personal login name and to ask for the relevant `#SBATCH` lines if the account name is still unclear.

ACTION

1) Ask the user to send the exact `#SBATCH -A` and partition lines from the script if they are not already visible in the ticket.
2) Clarify the distinction:
   - Login username is not the same thing as the Slurm charging account.
   - `#SBATCH -A` must use the Dawn project/account code associated with the user's access.
3) If the user already knows their project account from the DAWN portal or onboarding information, tell them to use that exact project code in `#SBATCH -A`.

4) Do NOT:
   - Tell the user to put their username in `#SBATCH -A`.
   - Create a new project/account unless project assignment is actually wrong.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Ruitian,

The submission error is most likely because Slurm expects the Dawn project charging account in the job script, not your personal login username.

Please check that your script uses the correct project account in the `#SBATCH -A` line. If you are not sure which project code to use, please send us the `#SBATCH -A` and partition lines from the script and we can check them.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep login identity and Slurm charging account separate.
- Do not guess a replacement account if project assignment is unclear.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Licence Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to load the lammps gpu module but is encountering an error message "sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account or account/partition combination specified" [1]. This error is often caused by an incorrect account or partition combination in the slurm script [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should check their slurm script for any errors in the account or partition specification [2]. They can use the "mybalance" command to check their available accounts and partitions [3]. If the issue persists, the user should contact the HPC support team for further assistance [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide your slurm script for review?
- Have you checked your available accounts and partitions using the "mybalance" command?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your email. I have checked my slurm script and found the error. I will make sure to correct it and try again. If I encounter any further issues, I will contact the HPC support team.
Best regards,
Ruitian

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should ensure that they are using the correct account and partition combination in their slurm script to avoid any errors or unauthorized access [2].
- The HPC support team should be contacted if the user is unsure about their account or partition configuration [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-57238
[2] : HPCSSUP-8291
[3] : HPCSSUP-15710

[CREATED] 2017-11-28T14:26:15.000+0000
[SUMMARY] job submission

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
I am trying to run a job on wilkes GPU for the first time. After I submitted the job using sbatch command, I got an error message
sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account or account/partition combination specified.
Do you know where could possibly have gone wrong?
I changed three places in the slurm_submit script: #SBATCH -A CHANGEME-GPU
Full path to application executable
and Run options for the application

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Julia,
We have simply copied the resources over from Darwin and Wilkes so you will only have a -GPU project if you had one already on Wilkes. If you would like one on wilkes2, please let us know what code you want to run and I will create one for you at the non-paying level.
Best regards
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Stuart,
I don't think I have any project currently running on any of the 
clusters. Could you explain the difference between Wilkes and Wilkes2?
I also got an error when trying to log onto HPC just now:
ssh: connect to host login.hpc.cam.ac.uk port 22: Connection refused
Do you know if there is an issue on the server side or is it a problem 
with my account?
Thanks,
Julia
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Julia,
Actually you have an old project on Darwin called IIDA-SL3, and this has been mapped to a new project on the new CPU cluster, called IIDA-SL3-CPU.
Wilkes2 is the new gpu cluster (part of CSD3), and Wilkes1 is the old. 
Wilkes2 contains 4 P100 GPUs per node, Wilkes1 contains 2 K20c GPUs per node.
There are a few more details available here:
[https://www.csd3.cam.ac.uk/services/csd3-platform](https://www.csd3.cam.ac.uk/services/csd3-platform)
The Darwin login nodes (login.hpc) are only accessible from inside the Cambridge network. If you are outside this, you can still ssh into the CSD3 login nodes (login-cpu.hpc and login-gpu.hpc).
Best regards
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Stuart,
I see what you mean. Could you create a project for me on wilkes2? I 
would like to do some machine learning training on the GPU cluster. Is 
there any other information I need to provide?
Thanks,
Julia
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Julia,
I have created a new IIDA-SL3-GPU project for you on Wilkes2.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Stephen,
As a matter of fact I do.
You're not a member of WALLACE-SL2-CPU. Only WALLACE-SL3-CPU and WALLACE-SL4-CPU. So you'll need to specify one of these to run a job, SL3 should be your default.
You can check which accounts you have access to by running:
mybalance
On the command line of one of our login nodes.
Best wishes,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Dominic,
Brilliant, thank you. That's done the job.
Warm regards,
Stephen

Dear Stuart
Sorry to trouble you again but I have one last question regarding to the 
new system. I saw from the website that there is a Peta4-KNL cluster 
which is available.

cheaper compare to that on Skylake. (as mentioned in the website one CPU 
on Skylake takes 0.32 pounds while one node on KNL takes only 0.14 
pound).
Could you please tell me if this is true? And also, can I run 
ABAQUS/matlab on the KNL cluster?
Many thanks
Best wishes
Burigede

<MESSAGE id=0019 role=HELPDESK_PRINCIPAL_INVESTIGATOR>
Dear Burigede,
The KNL cluster contains specialised Xeon Phi processes designed for highly parallel applications.
In fact, each processor presents 256 logical cpus on 64 cores. However, each cpu is relatively slow
and running general code on this is not recommended. Indeed, most applications would not be expected
to perform well, only those which scale sufficiently to use many cpus efficiently. It would make no
sense to run matlab on it, and probably not abaqus either.
You are welcome to try the KNLs in a non-paying capacity if you are interested, but I anticipate you
will be surprised at how slow they feel.
Best regards,
Stuart

> Stuart Rankin commented:
> 
> Dear Burigede,
> 
> The KNL cluster contains specialised Xeon Phi processes designed for
> highly parallel applications.
> In fact, each processor presents 256 logical cpus on 64 cores.
> However, each cpu is relatively slow
> and running general code on this is not recommended. Indeed, most
> applications would not be expected
> to perform well, only those which scale sufficiently to use many cpus
> efficiently. It would make no
> sense to run matlab on it, and probably not abaqus either.
> 
> You are welcome to try the KNLs in a non-paying capacity if you are
> interested, but I anticipate you
> will be surprised at how slow they feel.
> 
> Best regards,
> 
> Stuart
>

user_input:
Dear HPC Group,
I am Wenhao Xu, a PhD student in the Department of Clinical Neurosciences, working in Prof. Peter Smielewski’s group. I am writing to ask about extending our SL3 CPU allocation, as my current usage on account wx266 has reached the limit.
According to mybalance records, I have used 380,694 CPU hours under Account SMIELEWSKI-SL3-CPU over the last year, and I would be grateful if you could advise:
1.
Whether it is possible to request additional CPU hours for continued work this year; and
1.
Whether SL3 free usage reset annually, and if so, when the reset could occur.
If you need any additional details, please let me know.
Many thanks for your help.
Best,
–
Wenhao Xu
PhD candidate, Department of Clinical Neurosciences
Clare Hall, University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SL3 / CPU Credits / Service Level Policy
Technical Action Required: No
Finance Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports reaching SL3 CPU allocation and asks:
1) Whether additional CPU hours can be requested.
2) Whether SL3 free usage resets annually and when.

SL3 CPU credits are allocated at the project account level (shared across all users on that project). Credits are refreshed quarterly (not annually) at the start of each academic quarter.

ACTION

1) Clarify allocation model
   - SL3 CPU credits are assigned to the project account.
   - Usage is shared across all members of that project.
   - Individual users do not receive personal allocations.

2) Clarify refresh schedule
   - Credits refresh at the start of each academic quarter:
     • 1 February – 30 April
     • 1 May – 31 July
     • 1 August – 31 October
     • 1 November – 31 January

3) Direct to policy documentation
   - Service levels and SL3 policy page.

4) Do not:
   - Manually add credits.
   - Promise additional allocation.
   - Escalate unless there is a policy exception.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: SL3 CPU allocation and reset

Hello Wenhao,

SL3 CPU credits are allocated to the project account and are shared between all users who have access to that account — they are not assigned per individual user.

SL3 project credits are refreshed at the start of each academic quarter (three-month periods):
- 1 February – 30 April
- 1 May – 31 July
- 1 August – 31 October
- 1 November – 31 January

You can read more about SL3 service levels and policies here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels

Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply additional CPU hours can be granted outside policy.
- Do not treat SL3 credits as personal allocations.
- Do not promise resets outside the defined quarterly schedule.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC Account Management / SL3 CPU Allocation [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user, Wenhao Xu, is inquiring about extending their SL3 CPU allocation as their current usage has reached the limit [1]. They have used 380,694 CPU hours under Account SMIELEWSKI-SL3-CPU over the last year [1]. The SL3 service level provides 200,000 CPU core hours and 3000 GPU hours per quarter to each PI [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To address Wenhao's inquiry, we need to determine if it is possible to request additional CPU hours for continued work this year and if the SL3 free usage resets annually [1]. According to the documentation, each PI may receive 200,000 CPU core hours and 3000 GPU hours per quarter [3]. The credits on SL3 accounts are reset every financial quarter [7]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide more information about your research group's current CPU hour usage and anticipated needs for the next quarter?
- Have you considered purchasing additional CPU hours through the SL2 service level [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Wenhao,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your SL3 CPU allocation. We can help you with extending your allocation or provide information on how to purchase additional CPU hours. Please let us know your research group's current usage and anticipated needs for the next quarter. You can also consider purchasing additional CPU hours through the SL2 service level.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The SL3 service level is designed for groups with medium usage requirements who currently do not have funding to pay for their usage [5].
- The credits on SL3 accounts are reset every financial quarter to 200,000 cpu and 3000 gpu hours [7].
- The SL2 service level requires purchasing CPU hours independently of SL3 [7].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-39690
[3] : HPCSSUP-44145
[4] : HPCSSUP-100211
[5] : HPCSSUP-65502
[7] : HPCSSUP-66448

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-39690
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2021-10-29T10:53:52.851+0100
[SUMMARY] Computing services

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,  
I am writing to ask for your assistance with choosing a computing service for MATLAB jobs. I have a high number of MATLAB jobs (between 500 - 1000), and depending on the MATLAB script, a job can take several hours (in some cases > 5 hours) on a decent laptop. The scripts are not written in a way that the computations can be performed in parallel (other than MATLAB's internal functions). In my previous organisations, I used computation clusters (e.g., SGE) to submit many MATLAB jobs at a time. Is there such a computing service available at the University of Cambridge? I would be grateful if you could advise which computing service would most suit MATLAB jobs of this size.  
Many thanks for your help.  
Kind regards, 
Berk  
Muammer Berk MirzaResearch Associate Prediction and Learning Lab Department of Psychology Cambridge University

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Berk,
The 'CSD3' HPC cluster would be suitable for your needs I think. Matlab is used quite extensively to process data using it, I'd suggest applying for an account via the form at [https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application) giving the name of your PI, if you aren't one yourself.
This is a brief page from our user documentation on using Matlab on the cluster, once you have an account - [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/matlab.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/matlab.html)
We have versions between r2015b and 2021b available of Matlab currently.
There is a 'quick start' guide on using the cluster at [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html) but you can also ask any general questions via a ticket to [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk) as well.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>

Leon

Best regards,
Stuart

<MESSAGE id=0012 role=OTHER>
Dear Zacharoula,
The cpu hours under the SL2 service level (the paying service level)
are purchased in advance by your research group. Once purchased, they
may be consumed by any user who is a member of the associated project
(soga-sl2-cpu in this case). At the moment this only contains 35 cpu
hours, so only 35 further cpu hours of work in total may be submitted
against it. E.g. a single job using 1 cpu for 35 hours would empty the
project of core hours, as would 7 jobs (possibly from different project
members) each requesting 5 hours.
The only way to increase the balance is for your research group to
purchase more cpu hours to refresh the project, so I suggest you
consult your current head of group (I believe Krishna Soundararajan
made the last purchase, so presumably whoever replaced him would be the
person to ask).
Best regards,
Stuart

<MESSAGE id=0017 role=OTHER>
On Tue, 2019-09-24 at 07:07 +0100, Zacharoula Katsanevaki (Jira) wrote:
> I guess all this applies for SL2 usage. If I submit jobs for SL3
> usage, 
> I do not have this limit, is that right?
Every job is charged to a particular project. The available balance
associated with the relevant projects is the total limit that applies.
Use the mybalance command to display all your balances.
> 
> In my current sbatch script, the project to be charged is defined as:
> -A 
> SOGA-SL2-CPU
> If I want to change to SL3, do I just change it to: -A SOGA-SL3-CPU ?
Correct.
> Is there anything else I need to do?
SL2 projects are associated with Service Level 2; SL3 projects are
associated with Service Level 3. The project you charge the job to will
determine the service level according to whether the project has sl2 in
the name or sl3. The rest of the job parameters need to be consistent
with the limitations on the corresponding service level. Please see our
documentation for details on the restrictions applying to each service
level:
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels)
> 
> Also, what is the command to see my jobs in the queue so that I find
> out 
> how many are before me? Thank you.
Again please see our documentation but the commands you are probably
interested in are
squeue -u zk257
squeue -u zk257 --start
Regards
Stuart

Subject: RE: Advice on getting HPC quota from another lab group
Hi Pok-Man,
And a very happy new year to you too.
I’m a bit puzzled with this bit:
“we would like to find a way to let my HPC account accessing Marc's lab group HPC quota. Currently Marc's lab group has not sign up with the HPC, the same situation as Martin's when I started my years here in 2021.”
If Marc is not signed up to HPC, what quota are you then talking about?
Because I am very fearful that both Martin and Marc are running with old facilities in mind. So, up until 2022, they could get free processing and storage on the HPC (within limits) because the School was paying the bill for them. The ideal of the School scheme was to give our Departments the change to experience HPC and see if it works well, and delivers on its promises with how fast you can process on it etc.
But that scheme had ended. Now if anyone wants to process on the HPC (and they have both memory processing and CPU processing) then you need to buy hours on the HPC to do that.
So, If Marc does not have any CPU time on HPC for you to use, then this request does not make sense to me. IT would mean that you/Martin would need to buy your own time on the HPC.
I suggest you contact purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk<[purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk)> to discuss how much processing hours on CPU you think you would need so they can quote that for you. And your welcome to ask them if any free hours are available.
Regards,
Matthijs

Currently Marc's lab group has not sign up with the HPC, the same situation as Martin's when I started my years here in 2021.
Do you know any method to make this happen? I will be in the Hopkins in the afternoon if you have any time availability. If Marc needs to be present, please let me know and I'll notify him ASAP.
Thank you very much for your great help!
Best,
Pok-Man
P.S. I'm now doing some comparative genomics, but the process is surprisingly cpu-intensive and now I know I'll use up the lab quota very soon. Very unexpected, as this is only the second time I run the analytics.

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Matthijs,
Credits on SL3 accounts are reset every financial quarter to 200,000 cpu and 3000 gpu hours. This is done one project level and so those hours are shared by users who have access to those. Please see here for more details: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels . We also offer higher SL2 trier for which hours can be purchased independently to SL3 : [https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/charges](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/charges) .

Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team
</MESSAGE>

From: Matthijs van den Bergh <mv510@cam.ac.uk<[mv510@cam.ac.uk](mailto:mv510@cam.ac.uk)>>

Subject: RE: Advice on getting HPC quota from another lab group
Hi Pok-Man,
And a very happy new year to you too.
I’m a bit puzzled with this bit:
“we would like to find a way to let my HPC account accessing Marc's lab group HPC quota. Currently Marc's lab group has not sign up with the HPC, the same situation as Martin's when I started my years here in 2021.”
If Marc is not signed up to HPC, what quota are you then talking about?
Because I am very fearful that both Martin and Marc are running with old facilities in mind. So, up until 2022, they could get free processing and storage on the HPC (within limits) because the School was paying the bill for them. The ideal of the School scheme was to give our Departments the change to experience HPC and see if it works well, and delivers on its promises with how fast you can process on it etc.
But that scheme had ended. Now if anyone wants to process on the HPC (and they have both memory processing and CPU processing) then you need to buy hours on the HPC to do that.
So, If Marc does not have any CPU time on HPC for you to use, then this request does not make sense to me. IT would mean that you/Martin would need to buy your own time on the HPC.
I suggest you contact purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk<[purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk)> to discuss how much processing hours on CPU you think you would need so they can quote that for you. And your welcome to ask them if any free hours are available.
Regards,
Matthijs

Sent: Thursday, January 11, 2024 8:59 AM
To: Matthijs van den Bergh <mv510@cam.ac.uk<[mv510@cam.ac.uk](mailto:mv510@cam.ac.uk)>>
Subject: Advice on getting HPC quota from another lab group
Dear Matthijs,
Happy new year! Hope you have got a well-rested time during the Christmas break!
I'm writing to seek for your advice on how to apply for using another lab group's HPC quota within the Biochemistry department.
I have got consent from Dr. Marc de la Roche, with the full support from my supervisor Martin, we would like to find a way to let my HPC account accessing Marc's lab group HPC quota. Currently Marc's lab group has not sign up with the HPC, the same situation as Martin's when I started my years here in 2021.
Do you know any method to make this happen? I will be in the Hopkins in the afternoon if you have any time availability. If Marc needs to be present, please let me know and I'll notify him ASAP.
Thank you very much for your great help!
Best,
Pok-Man
P.S. I'm now doing some comparative genomics, but the process is surprisingly cpu-intensive and now I know I'll use up the lab quota very soon. Very unexpected, as this is only the second time I run the analytics.

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Open OnDemand / Jupyter / Service Disruption
Technical Action Required: Possibly (service-side investigation)
Escalation Required: Yes (if filesystem/service incident ongoing)
Urgency: Medium (interactive workflows blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that OnDemand Jupyter notebooks reach “Starting” state and then crash. The output log shows:

- `module: command not found`
- `jupyter: command not found`
- Timeout waiting for notebook port

These errors normally indicate that:
- The module environment was not initialised properly, or
- The underlying filesystem/environment (e.g. RDS) was unavailable at session start.

Because multiple core commands are missing inside an OnDemand-generated script, helpdesk should first check for an active service incident before asking the user to debug their own notebook configuration.

ACTION (HELPDESK)

1) Check for active incidents
   - Verify whether RDS or related shared filesystems are degraded.
   - Confirm whether other users are reporting similar failures.
   - Check service status / internal comms.

2) Do NOT ask the user to debug their environment immediately
   - The presence of multiple `module: command not found` entries in an OnDemand-generated script strongly suggests an environment failure rather than a user error.
   - Avoid requesting script changes unless no incident exists.

3) If a disruption is confirmed
   - Inform the user that a service issue is being investigated.
   - Provide reassurance that notebooks should function again once the service issue is cleared.

4) If no incident is active (alternate branch)
   - Check:
       echo $MODULEPATH
       which module
       module list
       which jupyter
   - Confirm correct partition/environment in OnDemand form.
   - Escalate to platform admins if environment initialisation is broken.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Interactive Jupyter Notebooks crashing

Hello Tim,

The log suggests that the OnDemand environment failed to initialise correctly rather than that there is a notebook configuration error on your side.

We are checking whether there is a current service issue affecting the shared environment or filesystem. We will update you as soon as we have confirmed that.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not attribute failure to user configuration when a known service disruption exists.
- Do not suggest modifying system scripts under /ondemand.
- Avoid unnecessary troubleshooting steps during confirmed incidents.
- If repeated reports occur without an active incident, escalate to platform administrators.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter Notebook / OnDemand [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: Yes (OnDemand) [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user's Jupyter Notebook interactive sessions are crashing before they can start, with error messages indicating that the `module` command is not found and the Jupyter Notebook server is unable to open the specified port [1][2]. This issue is similar to previous cases where users have experienced problems with Jupyter Notebooks on the CSD3 cluster [3][4][5][6][7][8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check the user's OnDemand settings and job submission scripts to ensure that the correct modules and partitions are being used [2][5].
2. Verify that the user has the necessary permissions and access to the required resources [1][3].
3. Investigate the possibility of a conflict with other jobs or sessions running on the same node [4][6].
4. Consider restarting the Jupyter Notebook server or trying a different node [7][8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about your OnDemand settings and job submission scripts?
- Have you tried running your Jupyter Notebook on a different node or partition?
- Are you experiencing any other issues with your CSD3 account or resources?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting this issue. We will investigate the problem and work to resolve it as soon as possible. In the meantime, please try restarting your Jupyter Notebook server or trying a different node. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all jobs and sessions are properly configured and submitted to avoid conflicts and resource issues [1][2].
- Verify user permissions and access to required resources before attempting to run Jupyter Notebooks [1][3].

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Ellie,
Sorry to hear that you are having trouble setting up your virtual environments in Python- I have taken a look through this ticket and I have a solution which has been tested and works. To begin with, I think we should switch over to miniconda so if you have not done so already then please visit https://www.anaconda.com/docs/getting-started/miniconda/install#linux  and follow the setup instructions. This is purely so that we can 1) manage the venv easier and; 2) follow the instructions from the [tcbench documentation](https://github.com/tcbenchstack/tcbench/tree/main). 
The first step is to create the venv and install tcbench. To so, simply run this block from the docs:
```
conda create -n tcbench python=3.10 pip
conda activate tcbench
python -m pip install tcbench
```
From this venv on the login node, I will then run:
```
pip install jupyter 
pip install ipykernel 
```
Once this finishes installing (it may take a little while), please run 
```
ipython kernel install --user --name=tcbench #<-- put here the name of your venv
```
To create the new jupyter venv. 
Open your web browser (whilst still connected to login node) and start a Jupyter session. For this section, please follow [our docs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments). Press "new", then in the drop down menu, hit `tcbench`:
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
Open your Jupyter notebook then type
```
import tcbench as tcb
root_folder = tcb.get_datasets_root_folder()
root_folder
```
If all this worked then you will see:
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
NB: you no longer need to use the `jupyter-env` you created. To use this venv, simply run `conda activate tcbench`. Since we require Python 3.10, Miniconda is the only way to implement this solution.
If you wish to use Jupyter how you were before the login node restrictions then you could always consider launching an interactive job: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr. Note that if you remove the `--qos=INTR` flag then you can set the job to run for as long as you need. 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support

In the past few hours, for the 2-3 notebooks that I have been able to load (after spending a few minutes in the “Starting” phase), the kernel has failed to connect to my cecilia environment (and even to the base environment, which I have also tried for comparison purposes).
I have now managed to load another GPU-based notebook (so at least, I’ve gone past the “Starting” phase), and it seems like the notebook has connected to my cecilia kerne (for the first time in more than a day!)! It’s taking a while to load all my packages… but this seems more promising, so I’ll keep you posted!
Please let me know if I you need any additional information or if I can help in any other way. Thank you so much once again for your time and support; I am deeply grateful.
Kind regards,
Mariona

Dear Ayden,

After it fails to open the notebook can you click on the Session ID and find the output.log file? If so can you please send me the file so I can investigate the cause of this issue.
For your second question in which node are you running the notebook? What do you mean the SLURM resources show as allocated? In order you run the notebook on a compute node you first get a compute or gpu node with srun or sintr from a SSH session then you start Jupyter on the compute/gpu node as usual and finally you port forward through a new SSH connection from your local machine to the compute/gpu node the notebook is running. See our instructions here: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/jupyter.html#running-jupyter-on-a-compute-node  . Once all that is done you can connect to the notebook from your local browser and any scripts will run on the remote compute/gpu node, you don’t need to run “srun” or any other slurm commands from within the notebook.
Kind regards,
Avraam

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation / GPU (Ampere)
Technical Action Required: No (user to retry with updated stack)
Escalation Required: Possibly (if issue persists after stack update)
Urgency: Low–Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports CUDA driver errors when compiling a newer version of LAMMPS with the GPU package on Ampere. The older stable version works.

Likely cause: mismatch between CUDA/toolchain versions and the currently loaded software stack.

ACTION

Ask the user to retry compilation using the updated module stack:

    module load rhel8/ampere/base
    module load gcc/14.3.0/vlhhcp6m
    module load cmake/3.31.10/gcc/7ddsybx7
    module load cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab
    module load openmpi/4.1.8/gcc/hemliivg

Then re-run the build and benchmark test.

If the issue persists after loading the updated stack, request:
- Full build output
- Exact LAMMPS version
- Full error message from runtime test

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Max,

Could you please try compiling again using the newest Ampere software stack:

  module load rhel8/ampere/base
  module load gcc/14.3.0/vlhhcp6m
  module load cmake/3.31.10/gcc/7ddsybx7
  module load cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab
  module load openmpi/4.1.8/gcc/hemliivg

After loading these modules, please rebuild and test again.

Let us know how you get on.

Best wishes,
HPC Support


SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not recommend installing custom CUDA drivers.
- Do not suggest modifying system-level GPU drivers.
- Keep troubleshooting limited to supported module stacks unless escalation is required.
response:

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to compile the newest release version of LAMMPS on Ampere with the GPU package, but is encountering a Cuda driver error [1]. The user has tried following the instructions on the CSD3 website, but is still experiencing issues [1]. The error message suggests a problem with the CUDA architecture or the compiler [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, we can try the following steps:
1. Check the CUDA architecture and ensure it is compatible with the Ampere GPU [1].
2. Try compiling LAMMPS with a different compiler, such as gcc/9 or gcc/11 [1][4].
3. Verify that the necessary modules are loaded, including rhel8/default-amp, cuda/11.4, and openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4 [1][4].
4. If the issue persists, try compiling LAMMPS on a different node or with a different version of the GPU package [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)

- Have you tried compiling LAMMPS with a different version of the GPU package or on a different node [1]?

[SUMMARY] Running LAMMPS on a single GPU

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
I have just recently begun running some experiments on the HPC cluster. I am trying to run a LAMMPS simulation similar to the example given at: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/lammps.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/lammps.html).
This example runs across all 4 GPUs in a node, and it works fine for me. I was wondering if it’s possible to reduce it to run on one GPU only? I have made the following update to the SBATCH headers in the slurm script:
#SBATCH -A GROSSER-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH -t 8:00:00
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --tasks-per-node=2
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH -o lammps.out
#SBATCH -e lammps.err
I also changed this value from 4 to 1 in the slurm script: -pk gpu 1
However, it doesn’t seem to run. I have read through the docs but I haven’t been abler to find the solution, so I would greatly appreciate if you could offer any advice.
Many thanks in advance.
Kind regards,
Emma

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Emma, 
As our docs page outlines: 
> …making use of all 4 GPUs on 1 compute node, running 2 MPI tasks per GPU thanks to the [CUDA Multi-Process Service (MPS)](https://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/mps/index.html) wrapper script detailed inside. This wrapper script allows to run efficiently and concurrently multiple MPI processes on a GPU by making use of the Hyper-Q capabilities on the latest NVIDIA cards. This allowing to optimise the GPU usage when each MPI process only use a fraction of the GPU memory available (80 GB on the Ampere cards). Without the MPS, the MPI processes will be run sequentially, wasting GPU time. 
Can you show me the error messages from this single GPU run? Ideally an err/out file. Thanks 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support
</MESSAGE>

Hi Emma,

HPC Support

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Andrés,
The latest gcc version for the login-e nodes (that you are using) is gcc/9. For the login-icelake nodes that are using a later version of CentOS the latest version of gcc is gcc/11 and gcc/11.2.0/gcc/rjvgspag. If you see any modules with gcc in the name, that is the version of gcc used to build the software. It doesn’t mean it’s the latest gcc version or that you should use the same gcc version to build any other software you need. You can find the available versions of a module with the command: “module avail 2>&1 | grep <module_name>”. Please try one of the latest version of gcc and let me know if you get any other errors.
For your reference the latest version of LAMMPS available on our cluster is lammps/23Jun2022.
Kind regards,
Avraam
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Yin,
Looks like our instructions maybe out of date. Can you try:
#download LAMMPS
git clone --depth=1 [https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git](https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git)
cd lammps
#load the CSD3 modules for CPU/KNL architectures
module purge
module load rhel7/default-peta4
#switch to the src directory
cd src
#activate the user-reaxc package
make yes-user-reaxc
#compile lammps with mpi support
make mpi
Kind regards,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Yin,
Can you try the following? I was only successful in making it compile with CUDA8, I hope this is sufficient.
#download LAMMPS
git clone --depth=1 
[https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git](https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git) lammps-gpu
cd lammps-gpu
#load the CSD3 modules for GPU architectures
module purge
module load rhel7/default-gpu
#build the gpu support library with P100 support
pushd lib/gpu
export CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/cuda/8.0
sed -i 's/CUDA_ARCH =.*/CUDA_ARCH = -arch=sm_60/' Makefile.linux
make -j -f Makefile.linux
popd
#load modules for the main make
module load gcc/9
module load openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4
pushd src
#activate support for EAM forcefields
make yes-manybody
#activate support for GPUs
make yes-gpu
#build lammps with mpi support
make -j mpi
popd
Kind regards,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Running LAMMPS on a single GPU

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Emma, 
As our docs page outlines: 
> …making use of all 4 GPUs on 1 compute node, running 2 MPI tasks per GPU thanks to the [CUDA Multi-Process Service (MPS)](https://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/mps/index.html) wrapper script detailed inside. This wrapper script allows to run efficiently and concurrently multiple MPI processes on a GPU by making use of the Hyper-Q capabilities on the latest NVIDIA cards. This allowing to optimise the GPU usage when each MPI process only use a fraction of the GPU memory available (80 GB on the Ampere cards). Without the MPS, the MPI processes will be run sequentially, wasting GPU time. 
Can you show me the error messages from this single GPU run? Ideally an err/out file. Thanks 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Provisioning / DAWN / Project Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (account provisioning once invitation accepted)
Escalation Required: Possibly (confirm invitation + provisioning state)
Urgency: Medium (user awaiting access)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is requesting access to DAWN and has provided name, email, and SSH key.

Correct process for DAWN access:
1) User must be invited to the DAWN project by the PI via Waldur.
2) User must accept the invitation in the DAWN access portal.
3) Once accepted, provisioning can be checked and the local DAWN account can then be created through the approved workflow.

Helpdesk should NOT:
- Manually create accounts before invitation is accepted.
- Install SSH keys manually from email.
- Bypass the Waldur provisioning workflow.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm invitation status
   - Check whether the PI has invited the user in Waldur.
   - If not invited → instruct user to contact PI.

2) Confirm invitation acceptance
   - If invited but not accepted → instruct user to log into portal and accept invitation.
   - Provide documentation link.

3) If the user says they have already accepted the invitation
   - Check whether provisioning has started or whether a provisioning ticket exists.
   - Escalate internally if the invitation has been accepted but no account-provisioning progress is visible.

4) Do NOT:
   - Accept SSH keys via email.
   - Manually append SSH keys.
   - Share temporary credentials.
   - Create accounts outside the provisioning system.

IF USER PROVIDES SSH KEY
- Acknowledge receipt but do not install manually.
- Once account exists, user can install key themselves after first login.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jun,

You need to be invited to the DAWN project by the PI via Waldur. Once you have received the invitation, please accept it in the DAWN access portal before we can proceed with checking your login-account provisioning.

Please see this guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#users-accessing-open-on-demand-via-the-portal

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never manually install SSH keys received via email.
- Never request or handle private keys.
- Do not create accounts outside the approved provisioning workflow.
- Ensure invitation acceptance precedes account creation.
- Do not bypass Waldur/project-based access controls.

user_input:
Dear HPC support team,
I have received the email that the HPC login issue has been resolved. But since yesterday evening till now, I still cannot login via the web portal [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/). I wonder if this issue has been resolved or not.
Best,
Yiran
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / Web Portal / Browser Behaviour
Technical Action Required: No (user-side troubleshooting)
Escalation Required: No (unless issue persists across users)
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that they still cannot log in via:

  https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/

despite being told the login issue was resolved.

This is most consistent with:
- Browser caching/session persistence issues, or
- A stale redirect/session cookie.

No evidence of account-level authentication failure.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Rule out browser cache/session issue
   Ask user to:
   - Clear browser cache and cookies for login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk
   - Try an incognito/private window
   - Try a different browser

2) Provide forced redirect link
   Ask them to test:

   https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/nginx/stop?redir=/pun/sys/dashboard/

   This forces the nginx redirect to reset the login path.

3) Confirm behaviour
   - If redirect link works → likely cached session issue.
   - If both links fail → escalate to web portal admins.

4) Do NOT:
   - Reset password unless authentication failure is confirmed.
   - Modify account without evidence.
   - Assume global outage unless multiple reports exist.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Yiran,

Could you please try clearing your browser cache or opening the portal in a private/incognito window?

If that does not resolve it, please try logging in via the following link:

  https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/nginx/stop?redir=/pun/sys/dashboard/

This forces a redirect and can resolve cached session issues.

Please let us know whether this works.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply account compromise.
- Do not suggest credential changes unless authentication failure is confirmed.
- Escalate only if issue persists across multiple users or browsers.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login Issue [1][3]
Technical Action Required: Possibly [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues logging in to the HPC web portal, despite receiving an email stating that the issue has been resolved [1]. There are multiple resolved tickets related to login issues, but none specifically mention the current issue [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Request the user to try logging in again and provide any error messages they receive [1].
2. Check the user's account status and ensure that there are no password or 2FA issues [3].
3. If the issue persists, investigate possible causes such as IP blocking or account configuration problems [1][4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message you are receiving when trying to log in?
- Have you tried logging in using a different method, such as SSH?
- Have you recently changed your password or 2FA configuration?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with logging in to the HPC web portal. We apologize for the inconvenience. Could you please try logging in again and provide any error messages you receive? We will investigate the issue further and provide assistance as needed.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's account is properly configured and that there are no security issues [1][3].
- Follow established procedures for resolving login issues and escalating problems when necessary [1][2].

user_input:
Good morning,
I have a question regarding the cluster’s behavior during reduced power consumption periods. I’ve noticed that jobs submitted during these windows are accepted and scheduled, but they execute at a significantly lower speed. This has led to several jobs timing out before completion, resulting in a loss of compute hours and requiring expensive reruns (even when requesting the same amount of resources as previously successful jobs).
Is there a way to prevent jobs from starting if the cluster is in a low-power state? Specifically, I’m looking for a requirement or constraint I can add to my job scripts (e.g., a specific #SLURM flag) to ensure they only run when full performance resources are available.
Thank you for your help!
Best regards,
Clara
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Cluster Performance / Power Management
Technical Action Required: No (policy clarification only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User observes that jobs submitted during reduced power consumption periods appear to run significantly slower and may time out. They ask whether there is a Slurm constraint (e.g. a #SBATCH flag) to prevent jobs from starting when the cluster is in a low-power state.

On CSD3, Slurm does not start jobs on “degraded performance” nodes. Jobs are scheduled only when requested resources are available. Reduced power states do not cause Slurm to allocate partially performing resources. 

Apparent slowdowns are more likely caused by:
- I/O contention (e.g. high demand on RDS shared filesystem),
- General cluster load,
- Application-level variability.

There is no Slurm flag to prevent job start based on power mode, as power state does not change the allocated CPU/GPU specification.

ACTION

1) Clarify scheduler behaviour
   - Slurm will only start jobs once requested resources are available.
   - Reduced power consumption periods do not change the allocated hardware specification for a job.

2) Explain likely causes of slowdown
   - Shared filesystem (RDS) performance varies depending on demand.
   - High I/O workloads across users can impact runtime.
   - Jobs may therefore run longer than expected.

3) Provide practical mitigation advice
   - Ensure jobs request realistic walltime.
   - Consider profiling I/O behaviour.
   - If appropriate, prevent automatic requeue by adding:
       #SBATCH --no-requeue
     (only if user specifically wants jobs not to restart after interruption).

4) Do NOT:
   - Suggest that jobs are intentionally throttled.
   - Promise guaranteed full-speed operation during all cluster states.
   - Suggest non-existent Slurm flags related to power state.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Job behaviour during reduced power periods

Hi Clara,

The cluster power status does not affect how Slurm allocates resources. Slurm will only start your job once the requested CPUs/GPUs become available, and jobs are not started on partially performing hardware.

If you are seeing slowdowns, this is more likely due to general system load or performance variation on the shared RDS filesystem, whose performance can fluctuate depending on demand.

There isn’t a Slurm flag that prevents jobs from starting based on power state. If needed, you can ensure your jobs are not requeued automatically by adding:

  #SBATCH --no-requeue

Otherwise, we would recommend reviewing walltime requests and considering whether I/O demand could be contributing to the longer runtimes.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply cluster misconfiguration without evidence.
- Do not suggest unsupported scheduler constraints.
- Avoid attributing performance issues to “power throttling” unless formally confirmed by cluster administrators.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Resource Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with jobs running at a significantly lower speed during reduced power consumption periods, resulting in timeouts and loss of compute hours [1]. The user is seeking a way to prevent jobs from starting if the cluster is in a low-power state, specifically looking for a requirement or constraint to add to their job scripts [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To address the user's concern, we need to investigate the current power restrictions and their impact on job scheduling [7]. We should also review the user's job submission script to ensure it is optimized for the current cluster configuration [2][8]. Additionally, we can suggest strategies to minimize the impact of power restrictions, such as requesting fewer jobs with more cores each [7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your job submission script and the resources you are requesting?
- Have you experienced any issues with job scheduling or resource allocation in the past?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your concerns with job performance during reduced power consumption periods. We are currently investigating the impact of power restrictions on job scheduling and will provide guidance on optimizing your job submission script. In the meantime, please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be aware of current power restrictions and their impact on job scheduling [7].
- Ensure job submission scripts are optimized for the current cluster configuration [2][8].
- Consider strategies to minimize the impact of power restrictions, such as requesting fewer jobs with more cores each [7].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-73417
[2] : HPCSSUP-60781
[7] : HPCSSUP-89989
[8] : HPCSSUP-81953

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-73417
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2024-07-07T22:11:19.059+0100
[SUMMARY] SLURM: Concerning Queue Time

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
My name is Jacob, and I occasionally submit SLURM jobs to the Icelake CPU cluster.
Roughly 5 days ago, I submitted ~20 jobs to Icelake, each requesting 1 node, 16 CPUs, and a maximum time limit of 12 hours.  It's been 5 days, and my jobs still haven't run; they're sitting indefinitely in the queue:
[cid:d3e486ac-2b91-4104-bf38-8d7f36d648ab]
I've submitted similar jobs before, and I never had jobs stuck in the queue this long. These particular jobs are for an upcoming, time-sensitive deadline.
Thus, I wanted to ask you:
1.
Is there any way you can prioritise these jobs so they're run and I can meet my deadline?
2.
Is there any way I can estimate queue times before I submit future jobs?
I apologise for sending this enquiry, as I know this long queue time was unintentional. However, I hope there's something you can do to allow these highly time-sensitive jobs to be run...
Jacob

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Jacob,
From what I can see your jobs are running now.  You can look up estimated job start time by running `scontrol show job >job_id>` .

Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-60781
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2023-09-12T10:11:16.366+0100
[SUMMARY] Job submission

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi, could you help me to understand job submission?
When I started in march, I would queue jobs without any problems. Now it seems like I am waiting days for them to start.
Can this be explained? Is it the quantity of jobs I have submitted, the requested duration or something else? Most of the ones I have submitted since the start of August I have had to cancel, and I am concerned I might have a user restriction, or the SL2 isn’t SL2-ing quite as I expect it to.
The others in my team do not seem to have issues.
Thank you, Steve.

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
Can you share a job submission script so I can have a look at the resources you are requesting. During the cluster maintenance in July we decommissioned a large part of the cluster which might have caused longer queues times for the remaining partitions. We are in the process of adding more compute capacity in September and October which will create a new partition and hopefully reduce the wait times for jobs in the queue.
Kind regards,
Avraam
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi,
I ran someone else’s batch file and it ran instantly.
I noticed I had written the following in my batch script, I removed it and it seems to be working a lot better today. I am wondering if this requests a specific node when added to a script like this.

#SBATCH -c 1
Thanks,
Steve
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
This option restricts the amount of CPUs per task. This probably conflicted with the other directives in your submissions script and caused your job to remain in a pending state. I’ll close this ticket now since you managed to resolve it. Let us know if you have any other questions or issues by opening a new ticket with [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk)
Kind regards,
Avraam
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Ok thanks Avraam.
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-73766
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2024-07-15T13:17:19.287+0100
[SUMMARY] Slurm

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support,
I wouldn't normally ask, but is there possibly some issue with my jobs getting through the queue?
It may simply be that the queue is very busy, in which case sorry to bother you, but ordinarily I can get roughly one or two jobs per day (or at worst every other day) through the queue (SL3) running on icelake and/or icelake-himem nodes. But in the last couple of weeks, I've had just two 10-core jobs run on the icelake nodes, and literally zero jobs run on the icelake-himem (three 75- or 76-core jobs queing for 5 days, then killed by me and re-queued, then stuck in the queue for over a week). In the same period, my student reports that he's getting somewhat larger jobs than this through the icelake-himem queue at about his normal tempo, hence my suspecting that it may be more than just a busy time...

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Pier,
The cluster is highly subscribed at the moment and queuing times, particularly for jobs submitted under SL3 accounts, are very long.
Your request 12 hours on a single node is the maximum time which we allow SL3 users to run for. To improve your queue times it is recommended that you only request the time you need. I.e. if your code only runs for 2 hours, then requesting 12 hours is a waste of 10 hours, and has only made your job harder to schedule, a better request would be for 2.5 hours (to allow for run-time differences). 
Similarly, tuning your request for cores, nodes and perhaps utilising the array job function (if appropriate) can shorten queue wait times.
Obviously if you need a single node for 12 hours then there is little which can be done.
I hope this info helps, if you have any further questions, please let me know.
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Chen, 
No if the job completes then it won’t hang on the compute nodes. It’s generally best practice to request a wallclock time commensurate  with the amount of time needed for the job to complete. This helps get the job running quicker and reduces wasted compute time (which in the case of SL2 accounts will also conserve valuable resources). Thanks. 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support

[TICKET_SUMMARY] SLURM: Concerning Queue Time

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
My name is Jacob, and I occasionally submit SLURM jobs to the Icelake CPU cluster.
Roughly 5 days ago, I submitted ~20 jobs to Icelake, each requesting 1 node, 16 CPUs, and a maximum time limit of 12 hours.  It's been 5 days, and my jobs still haven't run; they're sitting indefinitely in the queue:
[cid:d3e486ac-2b91-4104-bf38-8d7f36d648ab]
I've submitted similar jobs before, and I never had jobs stuck in the queue this long. These particular jobs are for an upcoming, time-sensitive deadline.
Thus, I wanted to ask you:
1.
Is there any way you can prioritise these jobs so they're run and I can meet my deadline?
2.
Is there any way I can estimate queue times before I submit future jobs?
I apologise for sending this enquiry, as I know this long queue time was unintentional. However, I hope there's something you can do to allow these highly time-sensitive jobs to be run...
Jacob

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Job submission

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
Can you share a job submission script so I can have a look at the resources you are requesting. During the cluster maintenance in July we decommissioned a large part of the cluster which might have caused longer queues times for the remaining partitions. We are in the process of adding more compute capacity in September and October which will create a new partition and hopefully reduce the wait times for jobs in the queue.
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi,
I ran someone else’s batch file and it ran instantly.
I noticed I had written the following in my batch script, I removed it and it seems to be working a lot better today. I am wondering if this requests a specific node when added to a script like this.

#SBATCH -c 1
Thanks,
Steve

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
This option restricts the amount of CPUs per task. This probably conflicted with the other directives in your submissions script and caused your job to remain in a pending state. I’ll close this ticket now since you managed to resolve it. Let us know if you have any other questions or issues by opening a new ticket with [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk)
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE>

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slurm

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
I can confirm that cluster is currently very busy and unfortunately waiting times take longer. Please do not cancel your jobs as resubmitting puts them at the back of the queue. I would also suggest using smaller wall times and where possible less CPUs so Slurm can fit your job between longer runs.

Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Queue Wait Time
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that a short GPU job has been waiting much longer than usual. This does not by itself indicate an account or script fault; queue time can increase when demand is high, and queued jobs gain priority over time.

Cancelling and resubmitting is counterproductive because it removes the job's accumulated queue priority.

ACTION

1) Reassure the user that long waits can happen when demand is high, even for jobs that usually start quickly.
2) Advise the user not to cancel and resubmit queued jobs unless necessary.
3) If they want a rough estimate, suggest:
   `squeue -u <CRSid> --start`
4) Clarify that starting an interactive job does not change the priority of an already queued batch job.
5) Escalate only if there is evidence of scheduler malfunction rather than normal contention.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Kai,

From the information provided, there does not appear to be anything obviously wrong with your job submission. Queue times can change significantly depending on current demand, so a job that usually starts quickly may occasionally wait much longer.

Please avoid cancelling and resubmitting the job unless necessary, as that resets its place in the queue and removes any priority it has accumulated while waiting.

If you want a rough estimate of when a queued job may start, you can run:

  squeue -u <your CRSid> --start

Also, starting an interactive job will not alter the priority of an existing queued batch job.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not promise a fixed start time.
- Do not imply scheduler fault without evidence.
- Warn users that cancelling/resubmitting resets queue age.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Scheduling / Queue Time
Technical Action Required: No [1][2]
Portal Action Required: No [1][2]
Urgency: Low [1][2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user's job has been in the queue for 15 hours, which is longer than usual [1][2]. The job is requesting 20 minutes of GPU time on 1 node [1]. According to previous tickets, long queue times can be due to various reasons such as high cluster usage, job size, and priority [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

ACTION
Please be patient and allow the job to wait in the queue. The job will run when resources become available [1][2]. If the job is still not progressing after a reasonable amount of time, please reply to this ticket and we will investigate further [1][2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your response. I will wait for my job to run and reply if it's still not progressing after a few hours.
Best regards,
Jiakai Chen

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not cancel and resubmit jobs frequently, as this can lead to lower priority in the queue [1][2].
- Be aware of the cluster's usage and job sizes when submitting jobs [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

I am a relatively new user of the hpc cluster and have been lately facing long queue times for the jobs submitted with the ampere gpu. For example, a job with the following specifications will still be on the queue even after a day of being submitted:
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=32
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=03:30:00
I was wondering if this is something to be expected or if it’s to do with anything from my side?
Thank you for your time!
Best wishes,
Sofia
–
Sofia Kapsiani
PhD Student (Molecular Neuroscience Group)
Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
University of Cambridge
Email: sk2067@cam.ac.uk<[sk2067@cam.ac.uk](mailto:sk2067@cam.ac.uk)>

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Sofia,
There is nothing wrong with your account or jobs, except that they are at the non-paying service level and there is a lot of paying activity which has higher priority. 
Please bear in mind that each paying job can occupy its resources for 36 hours. Please be patient and don’t be tempted to cancel and resubmit - since all jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, that would be counterproductive. 
Your jobs will run when resources become available.
Best regards,
Stuart

Hi Stuart,
could you please have a look into our difficulties in running simulations with 64 GPUs. We need the 64GPUs to
run the multi-frequency simulations for which we got the time (and have been very clear about this in our proposal).  
It has basically become impossible to run these now even in the first third of the quarter
(the latest attempt of my graduate student Shikhar Asthana is currently in the queue for
14 days now I think). Girish Kulkarni and Laura Keating have reported similar problems.
Many thanks
Martin
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0013 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Martin,
I've just taken this ticket. 64 GPUs constitutes the entire DiRAC section of the GPU cluster, and I can see how slurm will continually delay such a large job under these circumstances (while there are other DiRAC GPU jobs present).
However, your original TA form would surely have been sizing resources in terms of Wilkes2 nodes and P100 GPUs. The Wilkes3, A100 nodes are considerably heftier - please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html)
In particular each node has 1TB of host memory, and each GPU 80Gib of GPU memory. Please can I check that you really need to take 16 nodes of this much larger type to run your code? It seems to me that the size of your jobs should be smaller on Wilkes3 and you should no longer be afflicted by this scheduling issue.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0014 role=OTHER>
Dear Stuart, Dear Mark, 
the simulations we are running are now multi-frequency, so we need the extra memory per GPU. As Mark will recall this was 
part of the discussion of the specifications for the DiRAC3 GPUs. In the end the extra memory per GPU was the argument 
that the number of GPUs did not need to be increased and which allows us nevertheless to (hopefully) make the gains 
proposed in our proposal for DiRAC-3 resources. Without effective access to 64GPUs as before we will not be able to do 
what we have proposed. 
Best wishes Martin

[TICKET_SUMMARY] waiting for an unusually long time for GPU job

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
To whom it may concern,
I have submitted a GPU job last Saturday under the account BOROVIAK-SL3-GPU and 3 days later my job is still queuing (job ID 24900886). This is very unusual for me because my GPU job is usually allocated within 1 hour. It seems that the job has always been ranked around 30 in the queue and no progress is made. I am suspecting this has something to do with the maintenance last Friday because before maintenance, I had never experienced such a long delay.
I am sorry for troubling you with such a trivial matter, but I am working on a project with a very tight deadline (next week). I am wondering if there are any other GPU partitions apart from ampere that I could use, or if there are something wrong with my submission script, which I have attached in this email. Thank you in advance for your help.
Yours faithfully
Yutong

[TICKET_SUMMARY] gpu long queue times

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support group,
I hope this email finds you well.
I am a relatively new user of the hpc cluster and have been lately facing long queue times for the jobs submitted with the ampere gpu. For example, a job with the following specifications will still be on the queue even after a day of being submitted:
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=32
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=03:30:00
I was wondering if this is something to be expected or if it’s to do with anything from my side?
Thank you for your time!
Best wishes,
Sofia
–
Sofia Kapsiani
PhD Student (Molecular Neuroscience Group)
Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
University of Cambridge
Email: sk2067@cam.ac.uk<[sk2067@cam.ac.uk](mailto:sk2067@cam.ac.uk)>

[TICKET_SUMMARY] HPC buisy?

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support,
I have some SL2-gpu hours and in the last days it happens to me that it 
takes more than 24h for a script to get started. Right now I have a job 
in the queue 16639857 that is queued for more than 36h and is on 
Priority. Is that normal or is that an error? In the last month 
(July-September) the submission was way quicker...
Best,
Thomas

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Weird queueing time for a ampere GPU job

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear System Admin,
I'm writing to enquire about some suspicious behaviour of a GPU job that I submitted on Wilkes. The id of the job is 51213406, and I was requesting 12 GPU hours for one single A100 GP. This job has been queueing for 5 days and 4 hours and has never started to run until I killed it just now. This behaviour seems quite suspicious, so I'm writing to see if any potential causes could be traced.
My username on the HPC is ql309, and the project I submitted the job with is HOLDEN-SL3-GPU. When I run "sacct -X  -j 51213406 -o Planned", it gives "5-04:08:24" as output. The exact slurm script I used for submitting the job can be seen at [https://gist.github.com/QianyiLiu309/41a2ffbc2fc1114c730c785846a2d7e2](https://gist.github.com/QianyiLiu309/41a2ffbc2fc1114c730c785846a2d7e2).
Since the HPC outage in March, I have never run anything on the HPC, and this is the first job I'm trying to run. Since it's just requesting one GPU, it makes me suspicious that my account is unable to access the GPU compute at the moment?
Best regards,
Qianyi

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Long waiting times for jobs on gpu nodes

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello, 
since start of the week I have noticed extremely long queue times for the gpu jobs that I am trying to submit. At first I though it had to do with ongoing maintenance but this seems to affect only the login nodes. 
Therefore, I wanted to ask if there was any issue with our access or if the queue times are normal. I sometimes have to wait days for the jobs to be executed, which generally slows down the pace of work a lot. The reason for the queue upon request is always given as : (priority), is our priority extremely low? And if so, is there anything we can do about it? 
Thanks for the information and best regards, 
Yannick Hollenweger

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Login / Password Clarification
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is attempting to SSH to login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk and reports not knowing their HPC password, stating they may never have set one.

On CSD3, there is no separate HPC-specific password for internal users. Authentication uses the user’s UIS/Raven password, followed by the appropriate CSD3 TOTP token for SSH access.

ACTION

1) Clarify authentication model
   - HPC password = UIS/Raven password.
   - There is no separate password to set during activation.

2) Clarify SSH login sequence
   When connecting via:
     ssh <CRSid>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk

   The user should:
     a) Enter their current UIS/Raven password.
     b) Enter their TOTP code associated with CSD3 SSH (typically labelled “CSD3:ssh” or similar in their authenticator app).

3) If login still fails
   Ask:
     - Have you recently changed your UIS/Raven password?
     - Do you have the correct CSD3 SSH MFA token configured?
     - What exact error message appears after entering password/TOTP?

4) Do NOT:
   - Reset password directly.
   - Provide or request passwords.
   - Create a separate HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC password

Dear Fay,

There is no separate HPC password to set up. Your HPC login password is the same as your UIS/Raven password.

When connecting via SSH, please enter your current UIS/Raven password when prompted, followed by the TOTP code associated with your CSD3 SSH login (usually labelled “CSD3:ssh” in your authenticator app).

If you continue to experience issues, please let us know the exact error message shown after entering your credentials.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never request or store user passwords.
- Do not imply a separate HPC password exists.
- Escalate only if authentication failures persist after confirming correct credential usage.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account / Login Issue
Technical Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble logging in to their HPC account and does not know their password [1]. The user has been instructed to use their CRSid and UIS password to log in, but is unsure if they have a password set up for their HPC account [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should try logging in with their CRSid and UIS password [2]. If they are still unable to log in, they should contact the HPC support team to request a password reset [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried logging in with your CRSid and UIS password?
- Have you previously set up a password for your HPC account?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To log in to your HPC account, please try using your CRSid and UIS password. If you are still having trouble, please contact us and we will assist you with resetting your password.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should always use their CRSid and UIS password to log in to their HPC account, unless otherwise instructed by the HPC support team [2].
- Passwords should never be shared or sent via email [1].
