On Mon, 1 Nov 2021 at 12:48, <[support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk)> wrote:
> Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
> facility. CSD3 is comprised of three distinct clusters:
> Peta4-Skylake - Intel Skylake and Omni-Path CPU cluster
> Peta4-KNL     - Knights Landing and Omni-Path many-core cluster
> Wilkes2-GPU   - NVIDIA P100 and Mellanox EDR IB GPU cluster.
> For more information on the service please see:
> [docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk) 
> Connecting
> ==========
> Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
> key to [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk) (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
> forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
> on the Subject line to this email.
> Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
> UIS password.
> All users should login via SSH to any of the addresses below:
> [login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk)
> [login-knl.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://login-knl.hpc.cam.ac.uk)
> [login-gpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://login-gpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk)
> login-cpu and login-knl will both take you to one of a set of
> OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
> work for the Peta4 clusters (Skylake and KNL). login-gpu will take you
> to a different set of Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes best
> suited for development work for Wilkes2-GPU.
> We are allowing unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but
> have protected them with fail2ban. This means that after repeated
> failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be
> blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
> The SSH host keys have the following fingerprints (which of these
> hashes you see will depend on your client):
> RSA
>     MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
>     SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
> ED25519
>     MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
>     SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
> As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
> latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
> Storage
> =======
> Each user has a hard quota of 40GiB on their home directory. This is
> provided by ZFS over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
> automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
> file by accident look in /home/.zfs/snapshot and browse the best
> snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
> directory).
> The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
> Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
> ~/rds/ for your project's new "work" directory (it will have the name
> hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
> directory. Please note that it is already possible to buy additional,
> larger RDS areas (some users have already done so), these are visible
> on CSD3 as on Darwin/Wilkes, also under the ~/rds directory.
> User environment
> ================
> CSD3 nodes run Scientific Linux 7 (a free rebuild of RedHat Enterprise
> Linux 7). We recommend that all application binaries be recompiled on
> the appropriate login nodes.
> The default environment on login-gpu and the GPU nodes is based on
> GCC 5.4.0 and OpenMPI 1.10.7. If you wish to compile instead in an
> Intel compiler/MKL/MPI environment please do
> module load intel/bundles/complib/2017.4
> The KNL and Skylake nodes use Intel OPA which require different OFED
> libraries, and have Xeon CPUs supporting AVX512. Both Skylake and KNL
> code should be compiled on the login-cpu or login-knl nodes which
> provide a matching environment (we don't recommend compiling natively
> on the KNLs as this is very slow).
> Modules are now being generated by spack. The module names contain
> hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the
> modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to
> search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search
> for existing builds using
> spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
> which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
> were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
> choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
> Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
> MPI was used) can be extracted with
> spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
> Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
> such as
> spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
> which returns the name of the corresponding module
> (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that is sometimes quicker to
> search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
> the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
> spack module find /i52euam
> (notice the / introducing the hash).
> Missing builds can be requested. (For more informaation about spack
> see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
> Submitting jobs
> ===============
> CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
> mybalance
> to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
> compute unit hours.
> On CSD3 we are using natural compute units for each component, i.e.
- on Peta4-Skylake we are allocating and reporting in CPU core hours
- on Peta4-KNL we are allocating and reporting in KNL node hours
- on Wilkes2-GPU we are allocating and reporting in GPU hours.
>  
> We have adopted the convention that projects containing Skylake
> projects will end in -CPU, while those holding GPU hours for Wilkes2
> end in -GPU, and projects containing KNL node hours end in -KNL.
> The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
> SLURM submissions either through
> #SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
> in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
> sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
> Peta4-Skylake
> -------------
>  
> Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
> #SBATCH -p skylake
> or
> #SBATCH -p skylake-himem
> and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
> tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
> skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 5980MiB of RAM per task, and the
> skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12030MiB per task.
> Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
> increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
> more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 5980MiB per task
> to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is natively available
> there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
> distinction between cpus and cores.
> Peta4-KNL
> ---------
>  
> KNL jobs require the partition knl (#SBATCH -p knl) and will be
> allocated entire KNL nodes. Each KNL node has 64 physical cores but
> presents 256 cpus via hyperthreading, has 96GiB DDR RAM plus 16GiB
> MCDRAM high bandwidth memory and has been configured in quadrant/cache
> mode by default (in cache mode, the MCDRAM works invisibly as cache).
> It is possible to vary the MCDRAM mode required at job submission time
- please use either --constraint or the equivalent -C sbatch option to
 select the mode.  We recommend using either
>  
> #SBATCH --constraint=cache
> or
> #SBATCH --constraint=flat
> Flat mode makes the MCDRAM visible as a second 16GiB NUMA zone. Please
> note that hybrid MCDRAM mode, or any NUMA mode other than quad(rant),
> are not recommended.
> Wilkes2-GPU
> -----------
>  
> GPU jobs require the partition pascal (#SBATCH -p pascal) and may
> request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
> done via the directive
> #SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
> where 1 <=  N <= 4.
> Each GPU node contains 4 Nvidia Pascal P100 GPUs, with 96GiB RAM and a
> single 12-core Broadwell processor.
> Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
> node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
> requesting more than 3 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
> job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
> rejects a job outright.
> The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
> SL2:
> 1280 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
> 64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
> 128 KNL nodes per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job.
> SL3:
> 320 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
> 32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
> 64 KNL nodes per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job.
> In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
> 12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
> type=cpu, gpu or knl) which allows a limited number of jobs to run
> when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
> As usual, please email [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk) if any problems are encountered.
> Kind regards,
> Research Computing Services

﻿On 12/06/2017, 12:56, "support@hpc.cam.ac.uk" <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk> wrote:
Your account on the HPCS has now been set up and the name of your login
account is hpckoh1. Please either ring us to collect a password on
(+ 44) (0)1223 763517
or email to this address a SSH public key (RSA, 2048 bits minimum) to use for
authentication.
Please don't hesitate to ask us directly by email to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk
or by phone if any of the following details need explanation or clarification.
Logging into the HPCS
—
Firstly, please ensure that any self-administered machines (e.g. laptops)
which you use to login to the HPCS are fully up to date with respect to 
operating system updates, antivirus and anti-spyware software.
We will need to explicitly allow external IP addresses access through our
firewall. Please email us the appropriate originating IP address for your
local network if you have not already done so.
You should be able to login to one of the login nodes of the cluster
simply by doing:
ssh hpckoh1@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
from a UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X machine, or from a Windows machine with Cygwin.
Alternatively, free clients for Windows implementing SSH shell access 
and file transfer respectively can be downloaded from:
[http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/](http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/)
[http://winscp.net/](http://winscp.net/)
On connecting for the first time you will be asked to accept the HPCS public
key. The correct fingerprint of this key will be reported as one of the
following values:
RSA
MD5:0b:ef:59:90:fb:13:4a:c9:56:82:7b:cd:4b:2b:e1:3b
SHA256:sSkVfzpwjwiFvxLcdPoDpN8IsN3kt0ZSywhDhPKZPAg
ECDSA
MD5:34:9b:f2:d2:c6:b3:5c:63:99:b7:27:da:5b:c8:16:fe
SHA256:HsiY1Oe0M8tS6JwR76PeQQA/VB7r8675BzG5OYQ4h34
ED25519
MD5:64:7c:7c:ff:05:9d:0e:dc:06:fe:f1:c2:10:37:7a:85
SHA256:wq9ljBfPa7lXXpQq+rk5JTBXLJO/kXjOc5A7rp4ENzA 
either:
If you see a different fingerprint at this stage, please CANCEL and contact
support@hpc.cam.ac.uk as soon as possible.
VNC connections tunnelled over SSH are another possible method of accessing
the HPCS - for more information please see:
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc)
Getting started
—
To get started, please review
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start)
If you choose to change the password, please be sure to change it to something
of comparable complexity. Protect all passwords as you would credit card
information.
Planned maintenance
—
There is a regular planned maintenance slot on Tuesdays. This is not always
taken up but if it is will be advertised in advance. Every reasonable effort
will be made to maintain uninterrupted service otherwise, but external events
such as power or cooling failures do sometimes occur.
Directory permissions
—
By default your home and scratch directories on the HPCS have no permissions
(i.e. no read, write or search) for other users. Your primary group contains
your account only. If you need to relax these permissions, e.g. to allow a
specific colleague access, we recommend you seek advice from the support help
desk unless you are a UNIX expert before attempting to modify the directory
permissions.
Email
—
The system requires the ability to send you job-related emails. Email sent to
your account on the HPCS from the HPCS will be forwarded transparently to the
email address provided on the application form. Users may also send email to
other users in the same way whilst on the HPCS, however the true destination
address for messages sent to you is not visible to another user, unless you
direct email to that user (in which case your message is necessarily given a
valid return address). The HPCS does not accept or process email originating
externally.
The support team also uses email to advise the user base of impending events
such as planned maintenance periods, or emergencies affecting service. This is
done using the University list server and by default all users are added to
the list hpc-user@lists.cam.ac.uk. This is a moderated list and although you
may opt out of this if you wish, we would prefer you did not.
X windows
—
Under UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X, X windows applications may display correctly to
your local machine right away, otherwise please try ssh -Y. These remarks also
apply to Cygwin under Windows (start the X server with startx first). Under no
circumstances should it be necessary to expose your local machine to evildoers
(and then every other machine to which you connect) by using the command xhost
(please contact us if you have display problems).
Free software providing SSH and X windows functionality for Windows can be 
downloaded from:
[http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/](http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/)
Other
—
Please note that you are enabled as a SL3 user in the NIK-ZAINAL-SL3/-CLINCLOUD
project.
For news, current system status and documentation please visit
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)
In particular, please see
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies)
which describes our usage policies, and the FAQ at
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq)
Please let us know at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if you experience any problems.

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Chris,
Access to CSD3 via Tier2 is strictly by SSH key authentication.
If SSH key authentication is new to you then I always recommend this DigitalOcean tutorial on how to create a key pair. [https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-set-up-ssh-keys-on-ubuntu-20-04](https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-set-up-ssh-keys-on-ubuntu-20-04) you'll only need to complete step 1, which will mint you a private (id_rsa) and public (id_rsa.pub) keys.
If you're using Windows I'd recommend looking at using MobaXterm and creating an SSH key pair with it's generator wizard.
Once you have a pair, upload only the public part to SAFE and this will get automatically added to your CSD3 account within 5 minutes.
You can then (hopefully) log in. A typical command would be:
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa [cs-moor1@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:cs-moor1@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk)
Or you can configure MobaXterm to use your key to login.
Best,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

----Original Message----
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk) 
Sent: Wednesday, July 5, 2017 10:35 PM
To: Wu Shaohua
Subject: New Cambridge HPCS Account (hpcwu1)
Your account on the HPCS has now been set up and the name of your login
account is hpcwu1. Please either ring us to collect a password on
(+ 44) (0)1223 763517
or email to this address a SSH public key (RSA, 2048 bits minimum) to use for
authentication.
Please don't hesitate to ask us directly by email to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk
or by phone if any of the following details need explanation or clarification.
Logging into the HPCS
—
Firstly, please ensure that any self-administered machines (e.g. laptops)
which you use to login to the HPCS are fully up to date with respect to 
operating system updates, antivirus and anti-spyware software.
We will need to explicitly allow external IP addresses access through our
firewall. Please email us the appropriate originating IP address for your
local network if you have not already done so.
You should be able to login to one of the login nodes of the cluster
simply by doing:
ssh hpcwu1@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
from a UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X machine, or from a Windows machine with Cygwin.
Alternatively, free clients for Windows implementing SSH shell access 
and file transfer respectively can be downloaded from:
[http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/](http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/)
[http://winscp.net/](http://winscp.net/)
On connecting for the first time you will be asked to accept the HPCS public
key. The correct fingerprint of this key will be reported as one of the
following values:
RSA
MD5:0b:ef:59:90:fb:13:4a:c9:56:82:7b:cd:4b:2b:e1:3b
SHA256:sSkVfzpwjwiFvxLcdPoDpN8IsN3kt0ZSywhDhPKZPAg
ECDSA
MD5:34:9b:f2:d2:c6:b3:5c:63:99:b7:27:da:5b:c8:16:fe
SHA256:HsiY1Oe0M8tS6JwR76PeQQA/VB7r8675BzG5OYQ4h34
ED25519
MD5:64:7c:7c:ff:05:9d:0e:dc:06:fe:f1:c2:10:37:7a:85
SHA256:wq9ljBfPa7lXXpQq+rk5JTBXLJO/kXjOc5A7rp4ENzA 
either:
If you see a different fingerprint at this stage, please CANCEL and contact
support@hpc.cam.ac.uk as soon as possible.
VNC connections tunnelled over SSH are another possible method of accessing
the HPCS - for more information please see:
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc)
Getting started
—
To get started, please review
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start)
If you choose to change the password, please be sure to change it to something
of comparable complexity. Protect all passwords as you would credit card
information.
Planned maintenance
—
There is a regular planned maintenance slot on Tuesdays. This is not always
taken up but if it is will be advertised in advance. Every reasonable effort
will be made to maintain uninterrupted service otherwise, but external events
such as power or cooling failures do sometimes occur.
Directory permissions
—
By default your home and scratch directories on the HPCS have no permissions
(i.e. no read, write or search) for other users. Your primary group contains
your account only. If you need to relax these permissions, e.g. to allow a
specific colleague access, we recommend you seek advice from the support help
desk unless you are a UNIX expert before attempting to modify the directory
permissions.
Email
—
The system requires the ability to send you job-related emails. Email sent to
your account on the HPCS from the HPCS will be forwarded transparently to the
email address provided on the application form. Users may also send email to
other users in the same way whilst on the HPCS, however the true destination
address for messages sent to you is not visible to another user, unless you
direct email to that user (in which case your message is necessarily given a
valid return address). The HPCS does not accept or process email originating
externally.
The support team also uses email to advise the user base of impending events
such as planned maintenance periods, or emergencies affecting service. This is
done using the University list server and by default all users are added to
the list hpc-user@lists.cam.ac.uk. This is a moderated list and although you
may opt out of this if you wish, we would prefer you did not.
X windows
—
Under UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X, X windows applications may display correctly to
your local machine right away, otherwise please try ssh -Y. These remarks also
apply to Cygwin under Windows (start the X server with startx first). Under no
circumstances should it be necessary to expose your local machine to evildoers
(and then every other machine to which you connect) by using the command xhost
(please contact us if you have display problems).
Free software providing SSH and X windows functionality for Windows can be 
downloaded from:
[http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/](http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/)
Other
—
Please note that you are enabled as a SL2 user in the COMO-SL2/KRAFT
project.
For news, current system status and documentation please visit
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)
In particular, please see
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies)
which describes our usage policies, and the FAQ at
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq)
Please let us know at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if you experience any problems.
Best regards,
Robert W Klima

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi hpc_ssh support,
I am writing regarding access to hpc account.
I tried to log in via ssh on the terminal:
ssh hpckoh1@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
but couldn't really log in with my password. Is it possible to reset the password?
Best wishes,
Gene
﻿On 12/06/2017, 12:56, "support@hpc.cam.ac.uk" <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk> wrote:
Your account on the HPCS has now been set up and the name of your login
account is hpckoh1. Please either ring us to collect a password on
(+ 44) (0)1223 763517
or email to this address a SSH public key (RSA, 2048 bits minimum) to use for
authentication.
Please don't hesitate to ask us directly by email to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk
or by phone if any of the following details need explanation or clarification.
Logging into the HPCS
—
Firstly, please ensure that any self-administered machines (e.g. laptops)
which you use to login to the HPCS are fully up to date with respect to 
operating system updates, antivirus and anti-spyware software.
We will need to explicitly allow external IP addresses access through our
firewall. Please email us the appropriate originating IP address for your
local network if you have not already done so.
You should be able to login to one of the login nodes of the cluster
simply by doing:
ssh hpckoh1@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
from a UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X machine, or from a Windows machine with Cygwin.
Alternatively, free clients for Windows implementing SSH shell access 
and file transfer respectively can be downloaded from:
[http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/](http://www.chiark.greenend.org.uk/~sgtatham/putty/)
[http://winscp.net/](http://winscp.net/)
On connecting for the first time you will be asked to accept the HPCS public
key. The correct fingerprint of this key will be reported as one of the
following values:
RSA
MD5:0b:ef:59:90:fb:13:4a:c9:56:82:7b:cd:4b:2b:e1:3b
SHA256:sSkVfzpwjwiFvxLcdPoDpN8IsN3kt0ZSywhDhPKZPAg
ECDSA
MD5:34:9b:f2:d2:c6:b3:5c:63:99:b7:27:da:5b:c8:16:fe
SHA256:HsiY1Oe0M8tS6JwR76PeQQA/VB7r8675BzG5OYQ4h34
ED25519
MD5:64:7c:7c:ff:05:9d:0e:dc:06:fe:f1:c2:10:37:7a:85
SHA256:wq9ljBfPa7lXXpQq+rk5JTBXLJO/kXjOc5A7rp4ENzA 
either:
If you see a different fingerprint at this stage, please CANCEL and contact
support@hpc.cam.ac.uk as soon as possible.
VNC connections tunnelled over SSH are another possible method of accessing
the HPCS - for more information please see:
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/remote-desktops-and-3d/darwin-turbovnc)
Getting started
—
To get started, please review
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/using-clusters/quick-start)
If you choose to change the password, please be sure to change it to something
of comparable complexity. Protect all passwords as you would credit card
information.
Planned maintenance
—
There is a regular planned maintenance slot on Tuesdays. This is not always
taken up but if it is will be advertised in advance. Every reasonable effort
will be made to maintain uninterrupted service otherwise, but external events
such as power or cooling failures do sometimes occur.
Directory permissions
—
By default your home and scratch directories on the HPCS have no permissions
(i.e. no read, write or search) for other users. Your primary group contains
your account only. If you need to relax these permissions, e.g. to allow a
specific colleague access, we recommend you seek advice from the support help
desk unless you are a UNIX expert before attempting to modify the directory
permissions.
Email
—
The system requires the ability to send you job-related emails. Email sent to
your account on the HPCS from the HPCS will be forwarded transparently to the
email address provided on the application form. Users may also send email to
other users in the same way whilst on the HPCS, however the true destination
address for messages sent to you is not visible to another user, unless you
direct email to that user (in which case your message is necessarily given a
valid return address). The HPCS does not accept or process email originating
externally.
The support team also uses email to advise the user base of impending events
such as planned maintenance periods, or emergencies affecting service. This is
done using the University list server and by default all users are added to
the list hpc-user@lists.cam.ac.uk. This is a moderated list and although you
may opt out of this if you wish, we would prefer you did not.
X windows
—
Under UNIX/Linux/Mac OS X, X windows applications may display correctly to
your local machine right away, otherwise please try ssh -Y. These remarks also
apply to Cygwin under Windows (start the X server with startx first). Under no
circumstances should it be necessary to expose your local machine to evildoers
(and then every other machine to which you connect) by using the command xhost
(please contact us if you have display problems).
Free software providing SSH and X windows functionality for Windows can be 
downloaded from:
[http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/](http://www.cygwin.org/cygwin/)
Other
—
Please note that you are enabled as a SL3 user in the NIK-ZAINAL-SL3/-CLINCLOUD
project.
For news, current system status and documentation please visit
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)
In particular, please see
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies)
which describes our usage policies, and the FAQ at
[http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq](http://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-help/faq)
Please let us know at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if you experience any problems.
Best regards,
Simon Flood
– 
The Wellcome Sanger Institute is operated by Genome Research 
Limited, a charity registered in England with number 1021457 and a 
company registered in England with number 2742969, whose registered 
office is 215 Euston Road, London, NW1 2BE.

user_input:
Hi,
We would like to archive some old projects on our platform. Is RFS the only storage option available, or is RCS available from within the SRCP?
Thanks
Tom
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Storage Architecture / RFS
Technical Action Required: No (clarification only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

FOCUS OF RESPONSE
User’s second query:

“My understanding is that somehow storage for SRCP is provisioned on RFS. Is this RFS dedicated hardware that is isolated for use by a particular platform? Or does it work differently? In which case, is there the option to have dedicated RCS hardware, which will be cheaper?”

QUICK ASSESSMENT
SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS-backed storage that is logically and operationally isolated per platform/project. It is not pooled with general-purpose RDS/RCS environments, and users cannot directly mount external RCS storage within SRCP. There is no model for attaching separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a cheaper alternative.

ACTION

1) Clarify storage model
   - SRCP storage is provisioned as isolated RFS shares associated specifically with SRCP projects.
   - These shares are separated from other storage systems and not user-configurable.
   - The isolation is by design due to SRCP security requirements.

2) Clarify hardware/cost question
   - SRCP storage is not dynamically attached from arbitrary RCS pools.
   - There is no option to provision separate “dedicated RCS hardware” for SRCP at a reduced cost.
   - SRCP environments have a defined architecture and approved storage pathways only.

3) If cost optimisation is the underlying concern
   - Suggest reviewing retention strategy (e.g., archive/export workflow outside SRCP if appropriate).
   - Explain that external storage (e.g., RCS) would require controlled export and re-import, not live mounting.

4) Do NOT:
   - Suggest bypassing SRCP isolation.
   - Imply that SRCP storage can be merged with other RCS/RDS resources.
   - Promise alternative hardware provisioning unless formally approved.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: SRCP storage architecture

Dear Tom,

SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS shares that are logically and operationally isolated for SRCP projects. These are separate from other RCS/RDS storage systems and are not directly accessible or mountable from outside the platform.

It is not possible to attach separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a lower-cost alternative. The SRCP architecture and its storage pathways are fixed by design due to the security requirements of the platform.

If you are looking to reduce storage costs, one option would be to export data from SRCP and archive it to a nominated RCS project outside the platform, but this would not be a live integration.

Please let us know if you would like to discuss possible workflows for archiving data.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Maintain SRCP security model integrity.
- Do not suggest storage sharing across security domains.
- Avoid speculative statements about hardware provisioning beyond documented architecture.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Archive [1][3]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][3]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is looking to archive old projects on the platform and wants to know if RFS is the only storage option available or if RCS is available from within the SRCP [1]. According to the retrieved documents, RCS is a viable option for archiving data [1][3]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To proceed with archiving the data, the user can set up a new RCS store and transfer the data from the RFS store to the new RCS store [3]. The user can use the storage portal to purchase the RCS license and manage the storage [2]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the estimated size of the data to be archived?
- What is the desired duration for the RCS storage?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To archive your old projects, you can set up a new RCS store and transfer the data from the RFS store to the new RCS store. You can use the storage portal to purchase the RCS license and manage the storage. Please let us know if you need any further assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should ensure that they have the necessary permissions and access rights to manage the storage [1][2].
- The user should also be aware of the costs associated with the RCS storage and ensure that they have the necessary funds to cover the costs [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-61311
[2] : HPCSSUP-66096
[3] : HPCSSUP-44043

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Sarah,
This is definitely something we can assist with.
We can do a capacity analysis of the directories that you have listed so that we can quote you for a new RCS space. Once that is paid for and created we can facilitate the movement of the data on our end.
Once the copy is complete we can delete the copied data from RFS to allow for the shrinkage. At this point we'll know the remaining usage and we'll round it up to the nearest TB and +1TB as you request. And this is the number we'll shrink the RFS license to in the portal, so then you can renew for the correct cost using the portal.
If things are getting a little tight for time, we can invoke the shrink of RFS in the portal earlier, and delay reflecting the changes on the file-system until all the data is in the correct locations. The portal is mainly for book keeping and doesn't directly interact with file system, so we can intervene in cases like this.
Regarding your RCS lifecycle question can you clarify what you mean by lifecycle? Are you referring to our plans for the lifetime of RCS as a service?
Best,
Dominic

Dominic Friend commented:
Hi Sarah,
This is definitely something we can assist with.
We can do a capacity analysis of the directories that you have listed so that we can quote you for a new RCS space. Once that is paid for and created we can facilitate the movement of the data on our end.
Once the copy is complete we can delete the copied data from RFS to allow for the shrinkage. At this point we'll know the remaining usage and we'll round it up to the nearest TB and +1TB as you request. And this is the number we'll shrink the RFS license to in the portal, so then you can renew for the correct cost using the portal.
If things are getting a little tight for time, we can invoke the shrink of RFS in the portal earlier, and delay reflecting the changes on the file-system until all the data is in the correct locations. The portal is mainly for book keeping and doesn't directly interact with file system, so we can intervene in cases like this.
Regarding your RCS lifecycle question can you clarify what you mean by lifecycle? Are you referring to our plans for the lifetime of RCS as a service?
Best,
Dominic

Dear HPC support team, 
Apologies for bothering you about this.
A couple of days ago, I added Victor (CC’d; CRSid: vj292) to our lab RDS (rds-djh1002-hodson-rds) and RFS-NFS (rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs) space. However, in the self-service portal he is still showing as “Provisioning”.
I just wanted to check whether there is anything we can do to speed up access to the data, or whether there is something we may have missed on our end.
Many thanks for your help.
Best wishes, 
Joanna
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS & RFS / Provisioning
Technical Action Required: No (user action pending)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (access pending)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
A new user (vj292) has been added to both an RDS and RFS-NFS project via the self-service portal but remains in “Provisioning” state. The most common cause of this status is that the user has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions in the UIS Self Service portal. Until this step is completed, the provisioning workflow cannot proceed and access will not be activated.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm provisioning state (internal)
   - Verify that the user appears as “Provisioning” on the relevant RDS and RFS projects.
   - Check whether Terms & Conditions acceptance is outstanding.

2) Instruct user to accept Terms & Conditions
   Ask the new member (vj292) to:
   - Visit: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/
   - Click on each relevant storage project:
       • rds-djh1002-hodson-rds
       • rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs
   - Accept the storage Terms & Conditions for each project.

3) Explain workflow
   - Once Terms & Conditions are accepted, a provisioning request is automatically generated.
   - HPC Support will then activate access.
   - All relevant parties will receive automatic email confirmation once provisioning completes.

4) Do NOT:
   - Manually override provisioning before Terms & Conditions are accepted.
   - Escalate unless provisioning remains stalled after acceptance.

IF PROVISIONING DOES NOT COMPLETE AFTER ACCEPTANCE

- Confirm acceptance timestamp in portal.
- Check whether an automatic provisioning ticket was generated.
- Escalate to storage administration only if backend provisioning fails after acceptance.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Provisioning status for new lab member

Dear Joanna,

Thank you for your message.

Victor’s access is currently showing as “Provisioning” because he has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions.

Could he please visit:

  https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/

and click on the relevant project links to accept the Terms & Conditions for each storage project?

Once this is completed, a provisioning request will automatically be generated and we will activate his access. All parties will receive confirmation by email once this has been processed.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the mandatory Terms & Conditions acceptance process.
- Do not grant access before provisioning workflow completes.
- Ensure both RDS and RFS projects are covered if both were requested.

No, the group ownership of the project root directory has to remain with the project managers. That doesn’t get changed.
rc2018 probably needs to log out and back in again to pick up the extra group membership. Getting them to run `groups` or `id` should show if their session has the new rds one.
If they were connected to a login node when they were added to the group, it can take an hour for the `sssd` cache on the node to expire. If they disconnected and reconnected back to the same node, they might have kept the same group membership.
If their session on the login node shows the right group membership, we might have a stuck `sssd` cache on one of the storage servers that will need a kick.  But much more likely that them logging out and back will fix it.
</MESSAGE>

Subject: Re: RFS group
Hi Wanne,
Thanks for discussing with the group, I hope it went down ok over all
- Storage Limits -> There is a set storage limit for the entire RFS (currently 1TB). This can very easily be extended as and when needed. So there is no specific user storage cap.
- RFS is an active connection, so the only way to connect to it is to map it as a network drive/location and access the files directly. Having said that, users could utilise software like RSync to sync between the Mapped Drive and a local folder on their PC, but they would need to be careful only to sync their files and not the entire RFS.
- You will need to send the invites through the Self-Service portal. If you like, I can do this for you. It might be good practice to do it yourselves within the group though. Entirely up to you.
Hope this all helps,
Kind regards

Hi Wanne,
Thanks for discussing with the group, I hope it went down ok over all
- Storage Limits -> There is a set storage limit for the entire RFS (currently 1TB). This can very easily be extended as and when needed. So there is no specific user storage cap.
- RFS is an active connection, so the only way to connect to it is to map it as a network drive/location and access the files directly. Having said that, users could utilise software like RSync to sync between the Mapped Drive and a local folder on their PC, but they would need to be careful only to sync their files and not the entire RFS.
- You will need to send the invites through the Self-Service portal. If you like, I can do this for you. It might be good practice to do it yourselves within the group though. Entirely up to you.
Hope this all helps,

user_input:
Hi,
I need read and write access to the rds project rds-bru20-ukbb with the project ID isCN5CtgAac. I don’t think it’s possible to add two data managers, but I don’t think I can upload files to that rds without write permission. How else can this permission be given? Thanks!
Best wishes,
Emilia
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS Permissions / Project Roles
Technical Action Required: No (permission governance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (write access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests read/write access to an RDS project and assumes only one Data Manager can exist. On RDS projects, multiple Data Managers are allowed. If the user is currently a Data User, write capability should be granted by project governance (Data Owner/Data Manager) either by role reassignment or by applying permissions according to RDS permissions guidance.

ACTION

1) Clarify role model
   - Data Owners can appoint multiple Data Managers.
   - Data Users cannot self-assign elevated permissions.

2) Direct user to project governance path
   - Ask user to contact the Data Owner or existing Data Manager for:
     a) role change to Data Manager, or
     b) permission grant aligned with RDS ACL guidance.

3) Provide documentation link
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Emilia,

Data Owners can add as many Data Managers as required on an RDS project.

If you are currently a Data User, you will need to ask the Data Owner or a Data Manager to either:
1) reassign your role to Data Manager, or  
2) grant the required permissions in line with the RDS permissions guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not change project roles/permissions without Data Owner or Data Manager authorisation.
- Do not imply Helpdesk can bypass project governance for access control.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Permissions
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
By default, RDS projects have two levels of users: managers and users. Managers have full read/write permissions, while users have read-only access to the root of the project [3][6]. To gain write access, the user needs to be added as a manager or have a manager amend the permissions to allow write access to the users group [3][6].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, we can either add the user as a manager or amend the permissions on the filesystem. The user can be added as a manager by the current manager or the project owner through the self-service portal [7]. Alternatively, we can amend the permissions on the filesystem using standard tools like chown/chmod or by setting ACLs [6].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are you currently listed as a manager or user on the project?
- Can you provide the full directory path of the project you are trying to access?
- Are there any other users or managers currently associated with the project?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the issue with write permissions on the RDS project rds-bru20-ukbb, we can either add you as a manager or amend the permissions on the filesystem. Please let us know which option you prefer, or if you have any further questions.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- By default, users have read-only access to the root of the project, and managers have full read/write permissions [3][6].
- Changes to permissions should be made by a manager or the project owner [6].

REFERENCE KEY
[3] : HPCSSUP-34408
[6] : HPCSSUP-51660
[7] : HPCSSUP-96370

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Hugo,
This is a default with new projects now. Apart from storage portal where managers add/remove users they also get to manage project directories to more granular level.  If you are the manager and want to open write access to user (or visitors) group then you can achieve this with few acl commands i.e.
setfacl -Rdm g:<name-of-group>:rwx /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
This also works for single users with small change:
setfacl -Rdm u:<name-of-group>:rwx /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
To list who has access 
getfacl /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
 
Regards,
Greg
RCS Team
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Hugo,
Is there anything else I can help with or shall I go ahead and archive this ticket?
 
Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team

Weizhe

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi,
You would make any changes on the filesystem itself, if you wanted to change the permissions on a directory then you could either use standard tools like chown/chmod to make the users group (rds-hirYTW1FQIw-users) the group owner of it, then set the group to have read/write permissions.
E.g.
```java
$ chgrp rds-hirYTW1FQIw-users <directory>
$ chmod g+rwx <directory>
```
Or amend the ACLs on it directly, which can be used to give a more structured set of permissions. The below would grant read/write permission to all users to existing and future files, even if the user who created the file explicitly tried to remove access via chown/chmod.
```java
$ setfacl -R -m d:g:rds-hirYTW1FQIw-users:rwx -m g:rds-hirYTW1FQIw-users:rwx <directory>
```
We can make changes for you as well, if you let us know on a support ticket what you need to do. Those request need to come from (or be approved by) a data manager or the overall owner of the project.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Jordi,
There hasn't been any recent activity on this ticket, so I'll mark it as closed now.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

Hi Hugo,
This is a default with new projects now. Apart from storage portal where managers add/remove users they also get to manage project directories to more granular level.  If you are the manager and want to open write access to user (or visitors) group then you can achieve this with few acl commands i.e.
setfacl -Rdm g:<name-of-group>:rwx /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
This also works for single users with small change:
setfacl -Rdm u:<name-of-group>:rwx /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
To list who has access 
getfacl /<path_to_project>/<project_folder> 
 
Regards,
Greg
RCS Team

user_input:
To whom it may concern,
I have submitted a job on the HPC, and it took almost 2 weeks to get scheduled. I was wondering if there were any issues with my setup. The job script I used is below.
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J job_gpu_1
#SBATCH -o /home/av670/logfiles/job_gpu_1.log
#SBATCH -A SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
echo "This job is running on: $(hostname)"
echo "Present working directory: $(pwd)"
echo "Job ID: $SLURM_JOB_ID"
echo "Job start time: $(date)"

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Service Level Priority
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (long wait time but system functioning)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports a GPU job taking almost two weeks to start and asks whether there is an issue with their setup.

From the job script:
- Account: SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
- Partition: ampere
- GPUs requested: 4
- Wall time: 12:00:00

There are no configuration errors visible in the submission script. The most likely cause of delay is scheduler priority and service level.

Key scheduling principles:
- SL3 accounts operate at the non-paying service level.
- Paying service-level jobs receive higher priority.
- Priority increases over time while waiting (aging factor).
- Large GPU requests (e.g. 4 GPUs) may experience longer queue times depending on demand.
- Cancelling and resubmitting resets priority and can increase overall wait time.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm no configuration errors
   - The script contains valid SBATCH directives.
   - Correct partition and account syntax used.
   - No obvious resource mis-specification.

2) Explain scheduling behaviour
   - Jobs are prioritised according to service level and fair-share policy.
   - Paying activity can temporarily delay SL3 jobs.
   - Jobs steadily gain priority the longer they wait.

3) Provide best practice guidance
   - Do not cancel and resubmit while waiting.
   - Consider reducing resource request (e.g., fewer GPUs) if flexible.
   - Use `squeue -u <CRSid>` to monitor queue position.
   - Use `scontrol show job <jobid>` to inspect priority factors if desired.

4) Do NOT:
   - Escalate unless there is evidence of scheduler malfunction.
   - Promise faster scheduling.
   - Modify account priority.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the Job ID?
- Has the job been pending (PD) the entire time?
- What does `scontrol show job <jobid>` report under Reason= ?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC Job Scheduling Delay

Dear Amogh,

From the information provided, there is no obvious sign of a problem with your account or submission script. Your job is running under an SL3 (non-paying) GPU account, and at times there can be significant demand from higher-priority paying accounts.

Please note that each paying job can occupy its allocated GPUs for up to 36 hours. Jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, so cancelling and resubmitting would be counterproductive as it resets that priority.

Your job will run when sufficient resources become available.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply scheduler malfunction without evidence.
- Do not recommend priority manipulation.
- Avoid suggesting users circumvent service-level policy.
- Reinforce correct queueing behaviour and expectations.

Based on your slurm script, I can see that you are trying to use 1 GPU (`--gres=gpu:1`) but you are exclusively requesting for one complete reservation of a node by using `#SBATCH --nodes=1`. Which is causing a delay in your job getting prioritized or queued up in the slurm scheduler. I would recommend that you cancel you last job and try to make changes to your slurm script. Another recommendation is to increase up the no.of GPUs to 2 so your job can be completed within wall time of 12 hours.
```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J NNconv_pool_esm650M_akg_FE
#SBATCH -A SANCHEZ-LOPEZ-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=25
#SBATCH --time=11:59:00
```

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Marti,
Thank you for sharing the output. I am sorry for the suggestion that didn’t seem to work in your case. Thus we can re-use the `#SBATCH --nodes=1`directive back in the original slurm script. Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.
```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J NNconv_pool_esm650M_akg_FE
#SBATCH -A SANCHEZ-LOPEZ-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --time=11:59:00
```
I can see that few ampere nodes are available (as of now) for your job to be submitted and processed for running:
```
[ma2225@login-q-2 ~]$ sinfo | grep -i ampere
ampere                up 7-00:00:00     21  maint gpu-q-[61-63,65-72,81-90]
ampere                up 7-00:00:00      7    mix gpu-q-[1,16,26,31,33-34,39]
ampere                up 7-00:00:00     39  alloc gpu-q-[2-15,17-25,27-30,32,35-38,40,42-47]
ampere                up 7-00:00:00      1   idle gpu-q-41
ampere-long           up   infinite      1    mix gpu-q-1
```
Now answering your questions:
what the i) --ntasks flag does (in gpu submission): 
It sets how many parallel processes you want SLURM to launch. In GPU jobs:
For single-GPU, single-process jobs (which yours is), set:
```
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
```
You only need more tasks if your code uses MPI or runs multiple independent processes (e.g., multi-GPU)
ii) what does the export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 command does (in gpu submission)?
This sets the number of CPU threads that parallel libraries (like OpenMP) are allowed to use.
Even though your main work is on the GPU, some parts of your code (like data loading or CPU-side tensor ops) may use CPU threads. If your job uses `--gres=gpu:1`, you'd typically match that by setting:
```
#! safe value to no more than 128:
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=25
```
We would safely use 25-32 threads compared to ideal proportional number of 32 CPUs per GPU. This ensures you're using all the CPU cores you've requested.
Reference: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html 
Please share the output of any further errors if any.
Thank You.

Regards,
Muhammad Ahmed
HPC Services
</MESSAGE>

Just a final suggestion: As this problem might be encountered by other people in the future and it is quite hard to detect, it might be useful to include this bit of information in the hpc documentation.

Best wishes,
Marti

Dear Austin,
Your submission script and the command are correct. You are using the free SL3 project and the ampere partition is quite busy at the moment which is the cause of the higher wait times. One way to reduce the wait times is to request fewer GPUs for less time. In this case your job might get scheduled quicker even if there are higher priority jobs above you only if your job doesn’t delay or prevent these higher priority jobs from running. So for example if you request only 1 GPU for 1 hour and the scheduler finds that your job can run without affecting other jobs in the queue, your job will be scheduled and executed before the other jobs in the queue.
Kind regards,
Avraam

Hi Richard,
Those jobs are definitely in the queue to run, but there is a large number of other jobs in front of them. They are currently scheduled to start on Wednesday/Thursday according to slurm.
```java
$ scontrol show jobid 60543467 | grep StartTime
   StartTime=2022-05-11T06:17:06 EndTime=2022-05-11T18:17:06 Deadline=N/A

$ scontrol show jobid 60606820 | grep StartTime
   StartTime=2022-05-11T11:15:00 EndTime=2022-05-11T23:15:00 Deadline=N/A

$ scontrol show jobid 60723417 | grep StartTime
   StartTime=2022-05-12T10:55:00 EndTime=2022-05-12T22:55:00 Deadline=N/A
```

These are in high demand and the ampere partition is running with 63 available compute nodes rather than all the 80 we have, due to faults and several reserved for specific tasks. That's going to be contributing to the longer time wait times.

[SUMMARY] Submitting jobs to the Wilkes2

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
 
                Yesterday night I submitted a job and I have notice that I has not still start running. Since it is my first job in the GPU cluster, I was wondering if I did any mistake writing the bash script. First, this is what I see in the waiting list:
 
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
 
Second, this is the script I submit using the SLURM-submit.wilkes2  template. In brief, I just change a couple of headings:
 
- #! sbatch directives begin here ###############################
- #! Name of the job:
- #SBATCH -J openmm_ompCpH4
- #! Which project should be charged (NB Wilkes2 projects end in '-GPU'):
- #SBATCH -A SUMMERS-SL3-GPU
- #! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
- #SBATCH --nodes=1
- #! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
- #! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
- #SBATCH --ntasks=4
- #! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
- #! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 3 cpus per GPU.
- #SBATCH --gres=gpu:4

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Weird queueing time for a ampere GPU job

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear System Admin,
I'm writing to enquire about some suspicious behaviour of a GPU job that I submitted on Wilkes. The id of the job is 51213406, and I was requesting 12 GPU hours for one single A100 GP. This job has been queueing for 5 days and 4 hours and has never started to run until I killed it just now. This behaviour seems quite suspicious, so I'm writing to see if any potential causes could be traced.
My username on the HPC is ql309, and the project I submitted the job with is HOLDEN-SL3-GPU. When I run "sacct -X  -j 51213406 -o Planned", it gives "5-04:08:24" as output. The exact slurm script I used for submitting the job can be seen at [https://gist.github.com/QianyiLiu309/41a2ffbc2fc1114c730c785846a2d7e2](https://gist.github.com/QianyiLiu309/41a2ffbc2fc1114c730c785846a2d7e2).
Since the HPC outage in March, I have never run anything on the HPC, and this is the first job I'm trying to run. Since it's just requesting one GPU, it makes me suspicious that my account is unable to access the GPU compute at the moment?
Best regards,
Qianyi

[TICKET_SUMMARY] gpu long queue times

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support group,
I hope this email finds you well.
I am a relatively new user of the hpc cluster and have been lately facing long queue times for the jobs submitted with the ampere gpu. For example, a job with the following specifications will still be on the queue even after a day of being submitted:
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=32
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=03:30:00
I was wondering if this is something to be expected or if it’s to do with anything from my side?
Thank you for your time!
Best wishes,
Sofia
–
Sofia Kapsiani
PhD Student (Molecular Neuroscience Group)
Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
University of Cambridge
Email: sk2067@cam.ac.uk<[sk2067@cam.ac.uk](mailto:sk2067@cam.ac.uk)>

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Marti,
I can see that your job submitted is pending for a while since 9th May. 
```
[ma2225@login-q-2 ~]$ squeue -u mg2179

JobId=9227334 JobName=NNconv_pool_esm650M_akg_FE
   UserId=mg2179(46669) GroupId=mg2179(46681) MCS_label=N/A
   Priority=245 Nice=0 Account=sanchez-lopez-sl3-gpu QOS=gpu2
   JobState=PENDING Reason=Priority Dependency=(null)
   Requeue=1 Restarts=0 BatchFlag=1 Reboot=0 ExitCode=0:0
   RunTime=00:00:00 TimeLimit=11:59:00 TimeMin=N/A
   SubmitTime=2025-05-09T18:11:19 EligibleTime=2025-05-09T18:11:19
   AccrueTime=2025-05-09T18:11:19
   StartTime=Unknown EndTime=Unknown Deadline=N/A
   SuspendTime=None SecsPreSuspend=0 LastSchedEval=2025-05-13T20:12:48 Scheduler=Main
   Partition=ampere AllocNode:Sid=login-q-2:65209
   ReqNodeList=(null) ExcNodeList=(null)
   NodeList=
   NumNodes=1-1 NumCPUs=1 NumTasks=1 CPUs/Task=32 ReqB:S:C:T=0:0:*:*
   ReqTRES=cpu=1,mem=250G,node=1,billing=1,gres/gpu=1
   AllocTRES=(null)
   Socks/Node=* NtasksPerN:B:S:C=0:0:*:* CoreSpec=*
   MinCPUsNode=32 MinMemoryNode=250G MinTmpDiskNode=0
   Features=(null) DelayBoot=00:00:00
   OverSubscribe=OK Contiguous=0 Licenses=(null) Network=(null)

   WorkDir=/rds/user/mg2179/hpc-work/bio/ML-halogenases/DS2-fullembed
   StdErr=/rds/user/mg2179/hpc-work/bio/ML-halogenases/DS2-fullembed/slurm-9227334.out
   StdIn=/dev/null
   StdOut=/rds/user/mg2179/hpc-work/bio/ML-halogenases/DS2-fullembed/slurm-9227334.out
   Power=
   TresPerNode=gres/gpu:1

```
Based on your slurm script, I can see that you are trying to use 1 GPU (`--gres=gpu:1`) but you are exclusively requesting for one complete reservation of a node by using `#SBATCH --nodes=1`. Which is causing a delay in your job getting prioritized or queued up in the slurm scheduler. I would recommend that you cancel you last job and try to make changes to your slurm script. Another recommendation is to increase up the no.of GPUs to 2 so your job can be completed within wall time of 12 hours.
```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J NNconv_pool_esm650M_akg_FE
#SBATCH -A SANCHEZ-LOPEZ-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=25
#SBATCH --time=11:59:00
```
Can you give it a try? Feel free to contact us anytime and share screenshots of the error or output files if any.
Thank You.
Regards,
Muhammad Ahmed

</MESSAGE>

Dear Avraam,
I have tried both of the following submission scripts when trying to request an interactive GPU session (from icelake login):
sintr -t 4:0:0 --exclusive -A KHALED-SL3-GPU -p ampere
srun -t 02:00:00 --nodes=1 --gres=gpu:1 --ntasks-per-node=1 --cpus-per-task=3 --partition=ampere -A KHALED-SL3-GPU --pty bash
But both result in queues of 24hrs + which is obviously not practical for an interactive session.

With slurm_scvi.sh being the file attached.
Thank you for any help!
Best wishes,
Austin
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
 (0.0 kB)

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Austin,
Your submission script and the command are correct. You are using the free SL3 project and the ampere partition is quite busy at the moment which is the cause of the higher wait times. One way to reduce the wait times is to request fewer GPUs for less time. In this case your job might get scheduled quicker even if there are higher priority jobs above you only if your job doesn’t delay or prevent these higher priority jobs from running. So for example if you request only 1 GPU for 1 hour and the scheduler finds that your job can run without affecting other jobs in the queue, your job will be scheduled and executed before the other jobs in the queue.
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE>

user_input:
Hello,
I have been trying to compile the following package on GPU for a Molecular Dynamics code called LAMMPS ([https://github.com/wcwitt/symmetrix/blob/main/pair_symmetrix/README.md](https://github.com/wcwitt/symmetrix/blob/main/pair_symmetrix/README.md)). Given that some of the listed requirements aren’t directly available on CSD3, I have downloaded and compiled CUDA 12.4.1 and CMAKE 3.29. Before compilation I run module purge, then load the following modules:
1) gcc/9   2) openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4   3) gcc/11
Gcc 11 is required to compile, therefore I have checked that it overrides the automatically loaded gcc/9 bundled with openmpi. Furthermore, I have checked nvcc –version to check CUDA is correctly included in my path, and the same for CMAKE. I then run the following cmake command:
cmake \
-B build-gpu \
-D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
-D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
-D CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER=$CUDA_HOME/bin/nvcc \
-D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$CUDA_HOME/bin/nvcc \
-D CUDAToolkit_ROOT=$CUDA_HOME \
-D CUDA_TOOLKIT_ROOT_DIR=$CUDA_HOME \
-D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED=ON \
-D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$(pwd)/lib/kokkos/bin/nvcc_wrapper \
-D CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -ffast-math" \
-D CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER=~/rds/rds-ukaea-ap002-mOlK9qn0PlQ/ir-dick3/codes/lammps-symmetrix/cuda-12.4.1/bin/nvcc \
-D CUDA_TOOLKIT_ROOT_DIR=~/rds/rds-ukaea-ap002-mOlK9qn0PlQ/ir-dick3/codes/lammps-symmetrix/cuda-12.4.1 \
-D CUDA_INCLUDE_DIRS=~/rds/rds-ukaea-ap002-mOlK9qn0PlQ/ir-dick3/codes/lammps-symmetrix/cuda-12.4.1/include \
-D CUDA_CUDART_LIBRARY=~/rds/rds-ukaea-ap002-mOlK9qn0PlQ/ir-dick3/codes/lammps-symmetrix/cuda-12.4.1/lib64 \
-D CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=80 \
-D BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
-D BUILD_OMP=ON \
-D BUILD_MPI=ON \
-D PKG_KOKKOS=ON \
-D Kokkos_ENABLE_SERIAL=ON \
-D Kokkos_ENABLE_OPENMP=ON \
-D Kokkos_ENABLE_AGGRESSIVE_VECTORIZATION=ON \
-D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=ON \
-D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=ON \
-D SYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
-D SYMMETRIX_SPHERICART_CUDA=ON \
cmake
This runs without any errors (except for a CMAKE FIND_CUDA routine deprecated warning, however this shouldn’t be an issue as cmake shows it has found all the correct CUDA libraries, hence the cmake flags for different paths). After running make on this cmake config the binary compiles without any errors. When I run the binary, I get the following error:
‘’’
[gpu-q-49:22268] mca_base_component_repository_open: unable to open mca_btl_openib: libosmcomp.so.4: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory (ignored) [gpu-q-49:22268] mca_base_component_repository_open: unable to open mca_btl_usnic: libfabric.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory (ignored) [gpu-q-49:22268] mca_base_component_repository_open: unable to open mca_mtl_ofi: libfabric.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory (ignored) -------------------------------------------------------------------------- Primary job terminated normally, but 1 process returned a non-zero exit code. Per user-direction, the job has been aborted. -------------------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------- mpirun noticed that process rank 0 with PID 22268 on node gpu-q-49 exited on signal 4 (Illegal instruction).
‘’’
This is from running on a single GPU on the ukaea-amp partition. I load my CUDA 12.4.1 compilation, the same openmpi module used for compilation of lammps and gcc/11. I can’t understand why exactly there is a problem here. Any help on this issue would be hugely appreciated!
Many thanks
Ashley Dickson
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation / GPU (Ampere, ukaea-amp)
Technical Action Required: No (user rebuild with supported stack)
Escalation Required: No (unless failure persists after rebuild)
Urgency: Medium (runtime failure on GPU partition)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User successfully compiled LAMMPS + symmetrix with a custom CUDA 12.4.1 toolchain, but runtime fails on ukaea-amp with:

- Open MPI component warnings (libosmcomp.so.4, libfabric.so.1 not found)
- Process exited on signal 4 (Illegal instruction)

Likely causes:
1) Incompatible Open MPI module (built for Skylake + RHEL7) being used on Ampere (Zen3 + RHEL8).
2) Instruction-set mismatch (e.g. -march=native during cross-compilation on login node).
3) Mixed CUDA/MPI/compiler stack leading to ABI/runtime incompatibilities.

Resolution is to rebuild using the supported Ampere test software stack with a consistent compiler/CUDA/MPI toolchain and disable native CPU flags when cross-compiling.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Ensure clean environment
   - Start a fresh shell.
   - Remove old build directory:
       cd /path/to/lammps
       rm -rf build-gpu

2) Load base modules
       module purge
       module load rhel8/global rhel8/slurm

3) Enable Ampere test stack
       module use /usr/local/software/spack/csd3/spack-modules/test/ampere-2025-06-01/linux-rocky8-zen3

4) Load compatible toolchain
       module load gcc/14.3.0/vlhhcp6m \
                   cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab \
                   openmpi/4.1.8/gcc/hemliivg \
                   cmake/3.31.10/gcc/7ddsybx7

   Rationale:
   - OpenMPI built for Zen3 and RHEL8
   - CUDA ≥12 satisfied
   - Consistent compiler/runtime stack

5) Reconfigure build
       cmake \
           -B build-gpu \
           -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED=ON \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$(pwd)/lib/kokkos/bin/nvcc_wrapper \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -march=znver3 -ffast-math" \
           -D BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
           -D BUILD_OMP=ON \
           -D BUILD_MPI=ON \
           -D PKG_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_SERIAL=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_OPENMP=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_ZEN3=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_AGGRESSIVE_VECTORIZATION=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_SPHERICART_CUDA=ON \
           -D SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF \
           cmake

   Important:
   - Disable -march=native (SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF) when building on login node.
   - Ensure CMake does not mix multiple CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER definitions.

6) Build
       cmake --build build-gpu -j

7) Runtime
   - Load the same modules used for compilation before executing.
   - Submit via Slurm on ukaea-amp.
   - Do not mix MPI modules between build and run.

EXPECTED OUTCOME
- No illegal instruction at runtime.
- No Open MPI component mismatch warnings.
- GPU-enabled LAMMPS executable runs on ukaea-amp partition.

IF ISSUE PERSISTS
Request:
- Full submission script
- Full build log
- Exact runtime command
- Output of:
      module list
      ldd <lammps_binary>
      ompi_info | head

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Issues with GPU compilation of a package

Hello,

The runtime failure is most likely due to a mixed or incompatible CUDA/MPI/compiler stack rather than the package source itself. Please rebuild the code using a clean environment and the current Ampere test software stack, making sure that the same modules are used consistently for both build and run.

In particular, please avoid carrying over older OpenMPI or compiler modules, and disable native CPU-targeting flags when configuring the build on a login node. If the problem persists after rebuilding, please send the full build log, your submission script, the exact runtime command, and the outputs of `module list`, `ldd <lammps_binary>`, and `ompi_info | head`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not recommend installing custom system-level CUDA drivers.
- Avoid mixing MPI modules from different OS/compiler stacks.
- Ensure consistent module environment between compilation and runtime.
- Do not suggest modifying system libraries under /usr.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with compiling and running LAMMPS on the CSD3 system with CUDA and MPI support [1]. The compilation process completes without errors, but the execution fails with warnings and errors related to OpenMPI and CUDA [1]. The user has tried different MPI modules and CUDA versions, but the issues persist [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, we recommend trying the following steps:
1. Load the correct MPI module: `module load openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4` [2]
2. Use the correct CUDA version: `module load cuda/12.4.1` [1]
3. Check the compilation flags and options: ensure that the `-std=c++11` flag is used and that the `CMAKE_CUDA_COMPILER` is set correctly [1]
4. Verify the MPI and CUDA installations: check that the MPI and CUDA libraries are correctly installed and configured on the system [2]

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
* Can you provide more details about the compilation and execution commands used?
* Have you tried compiling and running LAMMPS on a different system or with a different version of CUDA or MPI?

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
* Ensure that the correct MPI and CUDA modules are loaded to avoid conflicts and compatibility issues [2].
* Verify that the compilation flags and options are correct to avoid errors and warnings [1].

Subject: libosmcomp errors when running lammps
Dear Stuart,

I'm currently compiling a gpu enabled version of lammps on the head node of the mtg cluster.
I have managed to compile the code successfully but when I try running I get the following error:
$ mpirun -np 2 ../../build/lmp -k on g 2 -sf kk -pk kokkos newton on neigh half binsize 2.8 -in in.lj
error while loading shared libraries: libosmcomp.so.3: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
My understanding is that the library causing the error is related to opensm. Is this something I can fix by loading some extra modules?
I have successfully compiled this version of lammps on our gpu machine (diamond) without too much fuss, so I'm not sure what I'm doing wrong here.
If it helps I use the following modules for the compilation:
$ module purge
$ module load rhel7/default-gpu
$ module load nvhpc/21.1
$ module unload cmake
I unload cmake because I used a need a newer version than what seems to be available on the mtg cluster. I downloaded a binary for cmake 3.26.4, which seems to work ok.
Cheers,
Peter

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Peter,
If you are planning to run your code on one of the gpu nodes then I believe you will need to compile your code on one of those nodes using available modules. I can also see that as a default libraries cpu-c-1 has /lib64/libosmcomp.so.7 but icelake or ampere nodes have  /lib64/libosmcomp.so.5 .

Regards,
Greg
RCS Team
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Greg,
Thanks for getting back to me.
Thanks for looking at the libraries, does so.x refer to the version here? So so.5 is version 5 and so.7 version 7? If so does this mean that the libosmcomp.so.3 error is due to some version mismatch between the available osm libraries and the version of the nvidia hpc sdk do you think?
On diamond we installed the nvidia hpc sdk version 23.1 and didn't see this error, the nvidia hpc sdk versions available to load on the mtg nodes are all 21.x
Also to clarify, for the moment I am just trying to get lammps compiled on the head-node of the mtg cluster. This is purely for benchmarking but would be very useful for us.
I'm using the headnode because it contains 2 K80 GPUs and there are published benchmarks for lammps run on these specific GPUs. Being able to compare directly to the published benchmarks on the same hardware will hopefully confirm that my lammps build is performing as it should.
Part of the reason for doing this is that I have compiled lammps on our GPU machine (diamond which has 2xQuadro-RTX-A6000) but the performance improvement is not quite as good as we expected, compared to the published K80 benchmarks and the specs for the GPUs ([https://technical.city/en/video/Tesla-K80-vs-Quadro-RTX-A6000](https://technical.city/en/video/Tesla-K80-vs-Quadro-RTX-A6000)). I want to run a range of system sizes on both, to make sure we're getting the expected performance out of diamond.
Cheers,
Peter
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Peter,
I think it is possible that different version of .so file was used for compilation. You can double check it’s actual version with something like:
`readelf -d /lib64/libosmcomp.so.5 | grep SONAME` which should return “real” name as sometimes libraries are re-linked i.e. “libosmcomp.so.5 -> libosmcomp.so.5.0.1”

Regards,
Greg
RCS Team

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Yin,
Can you try the following? I was only successful in making it compile with CUDA8, I hope this is sufficient.
#download LAMMPS
git clone --depth=1 
[https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git](https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git) lammps-gpu
cd lammps-gpu
#load the CSD3 modules for GPU architectures
module purge
module load rhel7/default-gpu
#build the gpu support library with P100 support
pushd lib/gpu
export CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/cuda/8.0
sed -i 's/CUDA_ARCH =.*/CUDA_ARCH = -arch=sm_60/' Makefile.linux
make -j -f Makefile.linux
popd
#load modules for the main make
module load gcc/9
module load openmpi/gcc/9.3/4.0.4
pushd src
#activate support for EAM forcefields
make yes-manybody
#activate support for GPUs
make yes-gpu
#build lammps with mpi support
make -j mpi
popd
Kind regards,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
You cannot just randomly stick builds together. What OpenMPI module are you using, it appears to be the wrong one for these, try
module load openmpi-1.10.7-gcc-5.4.0-jdc7f4f 
module load gcc-5.4.0-gcc-4.8.5-fis24gg
which corresponds to the GCC/OpenMPI default environment we have established for the wilkes2 cluster, but you must be consistent when you build you application.

Richard.

<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Adrian,
To get the best performance out of the coda-aware MPI and GPU-Direct, the module mvapich2-GDR/2.3-cuda9 is probably the best bet. For more information, please see our documentation at [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/performance-tips.html#gpu-direct-gdr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/performance-tips.html#gpu-direct-gdr) 
Hope that helps and please do let us know if we can help further.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

[TICKET_SUMMARY] FW: libosmcomp errors when running lammps

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Apologies I sent the below directly to Stuart rather than support
From: Peter Cooke
Sent: Friday, June 23, 2023 12:38 PM
To: Stuart Rankin <sjr20@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: libosmcomp errors when running lammps
Dear Stuart,
I'm a postdoc in Prof. Chris Pickard's group.
I'm currently compiling a gpu enabled version of lammps on the head node of the mtg cluster.
I have managed to compile the code successfully but when I try running I get the following error:
$ mpirun -np 2 ../../build/lmp -k on g 2 -sf kk -pk kokkos newton on neigh half binsize 2.8 -in in.lj
error while loading shared libraries: libosmcomp.so.3: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
My understanding is that the library causing the error is related to opensm. Is this something I can fix by loading some extra modules?
I have successfully compiled this version of lammps on our gpu machine (diamond) without too much fuss, so I'm not sure what I'm doing wrong here.
If it helps I use the following modules for the compilation:
$ module purge
$ module load rhel7/default-gpu
$ module load nvhpc/21.1
$ module unload cmake
I unload cmake because I used a need a newer version than what seems to be available on the mtg cluster. I downloaded a binary for cmake 3.26.4, which seems to work ok.
Cheers,
Peter

Paul,

Please note that if this was compiled on another machine, you should recompile it on CSD3; applications depend on many libraries which exist in different versions and in general binaries are not portable between different machines even if both are linux systems with similar cpus.

Stuart

[TICKET_SUMMARY] compiling/executing on csd3-cpu

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello, 
I am having an issue executing my code on CSD3. I can compile the code with no errors but when I attempt to execute it I get an error about a missing shared object library. The error is as follows:  
[cpu-e-215:374191] mca: base: component_find: unable to open /usr/local/Cluster-Apps/openmpi/pgi/1.8.8/lib/openmpi/mca_coll_fca: libfca.so.0: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory (ignored)
I believe that this is caused by loading the openmpi/pgi/1.8.8 module, which is not compatible with pgi/17.x. To resolve this, I have specified the openmpi/pgi/1.10.6 and pgi/16.10 modules, which I believe are compatible. However, I then get the compiler error: 
mpif90: error while loading shared libraries: libnvidia-ml.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
so it would appear I’ve just traded one issue for another. Could you please advise me on how to properly link these libraries? 
I am including my source code and related files. Within the included directory you will find a subdirectory for source code, and another for execution of the code. Within the source directory, the code is compiled with ‘make -f make_arms_csd3_pgi arms’. This creates an executable called arms. Then within the directory tri-null, the arms executable is submitted to the queue using ’sbatch submit-skylake.sh’. Finally, when the routine is executed, it creates a file called slurm***, which contains the error message that I mentioned. 
  
   
My list of loaded modules while compiling is set in my .bashrc as follows: 
module purge
module load dot
module load slurm
module load java
module load turbovnc
module load vgl/2.3.1/64
module load global
module load pgi
module load openmpi/pgi
and during execution a more limited set is called by the slurm file consisting of  
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh                # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge                               # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel7/default-peta4            # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment
module load pgi
module load openmpi/pgi
I’m not sure what, if any of this will be helpful in troubleshooting this issue, but I thank you in advance for your attention. 
Kind regards, 
-Roger Scott

Dundee, Scotland, DD1-4HN
