[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cuda aware MPI library

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear colleagues,
I am the PI on the DiRAC project DIRAC-DP035-GPU and I have recently obtained an account on CDS3 in order to begin using the GPU time awarded.
I am trying to run a simulation across 4 GPUs using MPI to communicate between the GPUs. Unfortunately, the code I'm using requires the MPI library to be "Cuda aware". When I compile, after loading the module rhel8/default-amp, I receive the error message contained in the screenshot that is attached to this message.
I wonder if it will be possible to install a Cuda-aware openmpi library to solve this problem.
To provide an example of where this has been done on another machine please click on the following link that shows how the code I am using has been run on the Jean Zay machine in France: [https://idefix.readthedocs.io/latest/reference/makefile.html#jean-zay-at-idris-nvidia-v100-and-a100-gpus](https://idefix.readthedocs.io/latest/reference/makefile.html#jean-zay-at-idris-nvidia-v100-and-a100-gpus)
For information, the code I am attempting to compile can be found in the directory
/home/dc-nels1/idefix/test/MHD/OrszagTang
and the code is documented here: [https://idefix.readthedocs.io/latest/index.html](https://idefix.readthedocs.io/latest/index.html)
I am using cmake to establish the required set up using the command shown in the file "cmake-command" in this directory.
I am then running "make -j 8" to compile the code.
Any assistance you can provide will be gratefully received.
All the best,
Richard.

[TICKET_SUMMARY] CUDA aware MPI

[CONTEXT]

Hi Jeffrey,
I did a bit of investigation on this. See below for the problems classified by environment.  Also could you compile an openMPI version with CUDA 10 so that I can use a newer gcc? I think the makefile is fine (it works in another two machines (Summit and Cooley) with no problem) but the problems are the following:
mvapich2 version:

    Have a look at lines around ~280. Those compilation options that I have not specified are being passed to the nvcc wrapper that’s why it is complaining.
   
OpenMPI version:
1) dot                              3) turbovnc/2.0.1                   5) singularity/current              7) rhel7/default-gpu                9) cuda/8.0
2) slurm                           4) vgl/2.5.1/64                     6) rhel7/global                     8) openmpi-GDR/gnu/2.1.1_cuda-8.0  10) gcc-4.9.4-gcc-4.8.5-3sdjf2c

Dear HPC team
I hope you are well.
I’m currently experiencing an issue when trying to start a new VNC session. The password window in the VNC viewer keeps blinking so I can not enter the password, and the connection never stabilises. I also tried to connect to the GPU node (gpu-r-4) directly to check whether a session was running, but the connection closes immediately after entering my password with the message: Connection closed by 10.43.79.4 port 22
It seems I am unable to access gpu-r-4 to create or manage a new session. Could you please advise on how to resolve this and re-establish a working VNC connection? Since I really need to finish some of the work and it just stopped working.
Thank you very much for your help.
Iryna
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: VNC / GPU node access
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance)
Escalation Required: No (unless multiple nodes affected)
Urgency: Medium (user blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports:
- When starting a new VNC session, the VNC viewer password prompt “keeps blinking” and they cannot enter the password.
- When attempting to SSH to gpu-r-4, the connection closes immediately after entering their password:
    "Connection closed by 10.43.79.4 port 22"

This may indicate a node-specific issue with gpu-r-4 or with the existing session on that node. The quickest mitigation is to start a new session on a different r node.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Advise user to start a new session on a different r node
   - Suggest an alternative node such as gpu-r-5.

2) If the user cannot start a session on any r node
   Request:
   - A full screenshot of the steps taken in the terminal (showing the command run and the resulting output).
   - Any error messages shown by the VNC viewer.

3) Escalation criteria (only if needed)
   - If multiple r nodes show the same behaviour, escalate to systems to check r-node availability/VNC service health.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Iryna,

It may be that gpu-r-4 is the problem for this session, so the quickest next step is to try a different r node.

Please set up a new session on a different r node (for example gpu-r-5). If you still cannot start a session, please send a full screenshot of the steps you are taking in the terminal (including the command and output) and any error messages from the VNC viewer.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not claim a specific root cause (e.g. SSH daemon failure) without evidence.
- Prefer the minimal workaround (use a different node) before deeper debugging.
- Escalate only if the issue affects multiple nodes or multiple users.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: VNC Connection Issue
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with connecting to a VNC session on the CSD3 cluster, specifically with the password window blinking and the connection not stabilizing [1]. The user has also tried connecting to a GPU node directly and received a "Connection closed" message [1]. Similar issues have been reported in previous tickets, such as [2], [3], and [4], where users experienced problems with VNC connections, including black screens, connection aborts, and password issues.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check the VNC password: The user should try resetting their VNC password using the `vncpasswd` command, as suggested in [6]. It is possible that the password is truncated to 8 characters, which could be causing the issue.
2. Verify the VNC connection settings: The user should ensure that they are using the correct VNC connection settings, including the display number and the GPU node, as shown in the `startgfx` output [6].
3. Try an alternative VNC client: If the issue persists, the user could try using a different VNC client, such as TigerVNC, to connect to the session [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS THE SITUATION FURTHER
- Can you provide the exact commands used to start the VNC session and connect to the running session?
- Have you tried resetting your VNC password or using a shorter password?
- Are you using the correct display number and GPU node to connect to the session?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To troubleshoot the VNC connection issue, please try resetting your VNC password using the `vncpasswd` command. Also, verify that you are using the correct VNC connection settings, including the display number and the GPU node. If the issue persists, you can try using a different VNC client. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When troubleshooting VNC connection issues, it is essential to ensure that the user is using the correct password and connection settings [1][6].
- The user should be aware of the potential security risks associated with using VNC and take necessary precautions to secure their session [5].

[5] : HPCSSUP-35953

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Nils,
To be able to connect to one of the gpu-r node with client like TigerVNC  you need to create SSH tunnel. Please take a look at instructions we have here:  https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/turbovnc.html#manually-creating-a-ssh-tunnel  . As an example for for gpu-r-6:5 this would be:
`ssh -L 5905:gpu-r-6:5905 nmb48@login-q-1.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
Where 5905 corresponds to port 5 you have your VNC server running. Once SSH tunnel is established use `localhost:5 `as the address to connect to with tigerVNC.

Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team

user_input:
Hello,
I have accepted the PI invitation for the project:
"Agentic AI Workflow Benchmarking and GPU Optimization for Verifiable Automation"
(Hypereum Ltd – UKRI AIRR)
My PI role is active and I can access the AIRR portal, however:
- The Dawn (A100 80GB) allocation is not visible under "UKRI Allocatable Offerings"
- I only see Isambard-AI offerings (IAG, IAI, IARA)
Could you please attach the Dawn allocation to my AIRR project and enable my Dawn user account provisioning?
Thank you!
Giovanni De Lillo
Principal Investigator – Hypereum Ltd
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: AIRR / Resource Allocation / Dawn Hardware Clarification
Technical Action Required: No
Portal Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (allocation visibility query)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that after accepting a PI invitation for an AIRR project, they cannot see a “Dawn (A100 80GB)” allocation under “UKRI Allocatable Offerings” in the AIRR portal, and instead only see Isambard-AI offerings.

The key issue is a misunderstanding of Dawn hardware. Dawn does not provide NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Dawn is based on Intel Data Centre GPU Max (Ponte Vecchio, PVC) hardware. Therefore, an “A100 allocation” would not be expected for Dawn.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Clarify hardware type
   - Explain that Dawn operates Intel PVC (Data Centre GPU Max) GPUs.
   - Confirm that NVIDIA A100 GPUs are not part of the Dawn system.

2) Direct user to documentation
   - Provide official Dawn hardware documentation link.
   - Encourage review of the hardware specifications to confirm suitability.

3) Do not:
   - Attempt to manually attach an A100 allocation.
   - Modify AIRR portal settings.
   - Escalate unless there is evidence of a genuine portal misconfiguration.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Giovanni,

There may have been some confusion regarding the hardware.

Dawn operates Intel Data Centre GPU Max (Ponte Vecchio, PVC) GPUs rather than NVIDIA A100 GPUs. As such, an A100 allocation would not be expected to appear for Dawn in the AIRR portal.

You can find details of Dawn hardware here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#hardware

Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply NVIDIA A100 hardware is available on Dawn.
- Do not suggest attaching incompatible GPU allocations.
- Avoid making portal-side changes unless a confirmed provisioning error exists.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: AIRR / Dawn Allocation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user, Giovanni De Lillo, is a Principal Investigator (PI) for the project "Agentic AI Workflow Benchmarking and GPU Optimization for Verifiable Automation" and has been added to the project in the AIRR portal [4]. However, the Dawn (A100 80GB) allocation is not visible under "UKRI Allocatable Offerings" and only Isambard-AI offerings are visible [4]. The user requires the Dawn allocation to be attached to their AIRR project and their Dawn user account to be provisioned.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the project details and the user's role in the AIRR portal [4].
2. Check the Dawn allocation and Slurm project configuration for the user's project [1][4].
3. Provision the Dawn user account and ensure the user has access to the Dawn resources [1][4].
4. Update the AIRR portal to reflect the correct Dawn allocation and Slurm project configuration [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide the exact project ID and user ID for verification [4]?
- Have you received any error messages or notifications related to the Dawn allocation or user account provisioning [7]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Giovanni,
Thank you for reaching out to us. We apologize for the issue with the Dawn allocation not being visible in the AIRR portal. We will verify the project details and user role, and provision the Dawn user account accordingly [4]. Please allow us some time to investigate and resolve the issue. If you have any further questions or concerns, please do not hesitate to contact us.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that all user accounts and project configurations are properly verified and provisioned to maintain security and access control [1][4].
- Follow the standard procedures for Dawn allocation and user account provisioning to avoid any errors or inconsistencies [1][4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-98329
[4] : HPCSSUP-95045
[7] : HPCSSUP-100066

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Stuart,
>Should this be associated with the “Supervised ML based spectral classification” project we already know about?
This would a separate project.
The project is part of my wider research programme of understanding and developing simulation-based inference tools for astrophysical data analysis. This requires training of classifiers, flows (diffusion and normalizing) and harnessing the latest advances in generative modelling for encapsulating probability distributions. My group currently applies these to cosmology and gravitational wave problems, but they are much more widely applicable than just to those fields.
I'd like my group to be able to access DAWN to reduce training times, and produce higher quality models scoped over a more systematic search of hyperparameters in order to disentangle some of the machine learning specifics from the general principles. At the moment GPU SL3 queuing times are the bottleneck in our research.
We would aim to share both the techniques and the trained models with the wider community.
Relevant papers:
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.15478](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.15478)
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06942](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06942)
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.02930](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.02930)
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.12841](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.12841)
[https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.16428](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.16428)
I would also aim to use this to inform a DiRAC allocation proposal in September.
Best,
Will

<MESSAGE id=0009 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Richard,
Here is a concrete project that James and myself would want to start working on ASAP.
1. Name: Will Handley, James Alvey
2. Department: Institute of Astronomy
3. Project title: Advanced GPU-Accelerated Inference for Astrophysical Data
4. Project summary: This project aims to leverage high-performance computing resources to advance our understanding of astrophysical phenomena. There are a number of aspects to the project. We propose to develop and implement foundation model methods for time series analysis, focusing on one-shot/fine-tuning models for astronomical data. This builds upon recent work that established scaling laws for such models ([https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.13867](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.13867)). This project used approximately 150 A100GPU days to establish scaling behaviour. We anticipate applying this to astrophysical data needing a similar amount of computing time, but depending on the resources available there would be considerable community interest in scaling this work to significantly larger amounts of compute. We would value discussing this further with DAWN. Additionally, we plan to explore GPU-based nested sampling techniques using frameworks like BlackJAX, with applications ranging from gravitational wave analysis to JWST data processing and cosmological parameter estimation. Finally, this project will investigate the related potential of GPU-accelerated simulation-based inference (SBI) methods, particularly for learning generative models of noise distributions in various astrophysical contexts, including LIGO timestreams and for LISA massive black hole binary signals.
5. Summary of compute needs: Our project requires substantial GPU resources for training and running large-scale time series models and performing nested sampling computations. As mentioned above, the pilot project used approximately 150 GPU days on A100 GPUs. We anticipate needing a similar or greater allocation on the DAWN system. Key software and libraries to carry this out include: PyTorch, BlackJAX for nested sampling, custom SBI libraries (e.g., swyft). We believe that oneAPI Intel GPU ports for these tools already exist (e.g. the swyft port has been tested recently). The project may also require significant data storage (O(2 TB)) for time series datasets and model checkpoints.
6. Level of familiarity with high performance compute systems: Our team has extensive experience working with HPC systems, including GPU clusters. We have previously utilized systems similar to Dawn for large-scale astrophysical simulations and data analysis. We are proficient in parallel computing techniques, GPU programming (mainly CUDA-based), and optimizing code for high-performance environments. However, we may require some guidance on Dawn-specific optimizations and best practices.
7. Type of support you might need: We anticipate potentially needing the following support: Support for any additional oneAPI Intel ports
8. Readiness: We are prepared to begin work on this project immediately. Our initial phase will involve porting existing code to the Dawn system and conducting preliminary scaling tests. We estimate that within 1-2 months, we will be ready to launch our first full-scale experiments. The project is expected to run for at least 12 months, with the potential for extension based on initial results and resource availability. In addition, the blackjax nested sampling code is already written and tested, as well as the swyft-based SBI algorithms.
Best,
Will

user_input:
Dear IT team
I am Alejo Nevado-Holgado, user "n_neva1" of Dawn. A couple of months ago I was trying to SSH into dawn from my laptop, but in order to do that I first had to create an SSH key with the following command:
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100 -C "dn_neva1@dawn (AIRR-p19)"
Then I sent the public key to this email address in a file called "id_ed25519.pub". A couple of days ago I reinstalled Ubuntu, and now I am not able to SSH into Dawn again. I didn't save the private side of the key that I generated before. I was wondering, how could I connect to Dawn again from my laptop? Claude says that I have to generate a new key and send to you the new "id_ed25519.pub" file. I attach it in case this would actually work.
Very best and many thanks for your help
Alejo
Alejo Nevado-Holgado
Associate Professor
Department of Psychiatry & Big Data Institute | University of Oxford | Oxford | OX3 7JX
alejo.nevado-holgado@psych.ox.ac.uk<[alejo.nevado-holgado@psych.ox.ac.uk](mailto:alejo.nevado-holgado@psych.ox.ac.uk)>
[cid:108711ff-f78d-4b60-b2a2-bb8b3941fc7a]  [cid:af762036-bceb-4b4b-a09e-6924cd209afa]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Keys (Dawn)
Technical Action Required: Yes (append public keys)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User lost the private key after reinstall and needs a replacement key registered. Each client device requires its own valid key pair, and the corresponding public key must be appended server-side.

ACTION
1) Append new public key for replacement laptop key.
2) Clarify per-device key model (one key pair per device).
3) If attachments fail, request public key pasted as plain text in email body.
4) If the user later needs access from another device, repeat the same process for that device's public key.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Alejo,

You will need a new valid SSH key pair on the laptop you reinstalled. Please send the replacement public key for that device (you can paste it directly as plain text in the email body), and we can append it to your account.

If you also connect from other devices, each device will need its own key pair and public-key registration.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never request private key material.
- Accept only public keys.
- Confirm key addition only after staff-side update is complete.

The user, Alejo Nevado-Holgado, is experiencing issues with SSH access to Dawn after reinstalling Ubuntu and losing the private key [1]. The user has generated a new key pair and is requesting assistance with adding the public key to their account [1][2].

ACTION
To resolve the issue, the new public key needs to be added to the user's account [1][2]. The user has already generated a new key pair and attached the public key to the email [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's identity and ensure the new public key is correctly formatted [1][2].
2. Add the new public key to the user's account [1][2].
3. Inform the user to try connecting to Dawn via SSH again and report any further issues [1][2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Alejo,
I have added your new public key to your account. Please try connecting to Dawn via SSH again and let me know if you encounter any further issues.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user's identity is verified before adding a new public key to their account [1][2].
- Only add public keys to user accounts, never private keys [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-75228
[2] : HPCSSUP-97179

user_input:
To whom it may concern,
We have a job on HPC that takes more than 36 hours to run, and we would like to try the check point method. Could you provide some examples of how to use the check point?
Thanks,
Qi
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Runtime / Checkpointing Guidance
Technical Action Required: No (advisory)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asks for checkpointing examples for jobs exceeding wall time. Provide concise, generic checkpoint patterns (periodic save + resume path), point to the linked reference, and clarify that implementation must be adapted to the user’s application and CSD3 environment.

ACTION
1) Explain user-level checkpointing concept.
2) Provide 1-2 generic checkpoint patterns the user can apply.
3) Provide external reference as conceptual guidance.
4) Clarify that commands/examples may need adaptation for local environment.

EXAMPLE CHECKPOINT PATTERNS (GENERIC)
1) Periodic state save + restart
   - Run application so it writes restart files every N minutes/iterations to a checkpoint directory.
   - On next job run, detect latest checkpoint and resume from it instead of starting from scratch.

2) Pre-timeout save + requeue/next run
   - Configure application (or wrapper) to write a final checkpoint before wall-time expiry.
   - Submit the next job to resume from that checkpoint file.

Generic wrapper pattern:
```
if [ -f checkpoints/latest.ckpt ]; then
  my_app --resume checkpoints/latest.ckpt
else
  my_app --start
fi
```

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Qi,

We don’t have a ready CSD3-specific checkpoint script to provide, but the standard approach is:

1) write restart/checkpoint files periodically during the run, and  
2) start the next job by resuming from the latest checkpoint.

Two generic patterns you can use are:
- Periodic checkpointing: save state every N iterations/minutes, then resume from the most recent checkpoint on the next run.
- Pre-timeout checkpointing: write a final checkpoint before wall-time is reached, then submit the next job to resume from it.

For example, your wrapper logic can be:

```
if [ -f checkpoints/latest.ckpt ]; then
  my_app --resume checkpoints/latest.ckpt
else
  my_app --start
fi
```

This overview may help:
https://rc-docs.northeastern.edu/en/latest/best-practices/checkpointing.html#the-checkpointing-technique

Please note these are generic patterns and may need adaptation for your application and CSD3.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply native automatic checkpoint support unless confirmed.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Checkpointing
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is looking to use checkpointing for a job that takes more than 36 hours to run on the HPC [1]. Checkpointing is a method to save the state of a job at regular intervals, allowing it to be restarted from the last checkpoint in case of an interruption [2]. However, the user is unsure of how to implement checkpointing.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To use checkpointing, the user can utilize the DMTCP (Distributed Multi-Threaded Checkpointing) tool [5]. However, it is noted that DMTCP is no longer supported [3]. An alternative approach is to manually save the state of the job at regular intervals, allowing it to be restarted from the last saved state [2]. The user can also consider splitting the job into smaller chunks or using a different queue with a longer time limit [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about the job you are trying to run, such as the code and input data?
- Have you considered using a different queue or partition, such as the long queue, for your job?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To use checkpointing for your job, you can try utilizing the DMTCP tool or manually saving the state of your job at regular intervals. If you need further assistance or have questions about implementing checkpointing, please let us know.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Checkpointing should be used to ensure that long-running jobs can be restarted in case of an interruption, but it may not be suitable for all types of jobs [2].
- The use of DMTCP is no longer supported, and alternative methods should be explored [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-99690
[2] : HPCSSUP-50313
[3] : HPCSSUP-44885
[4] : HPCSSUP-23904
[5] : HPCSSUP-19366

Heather

Department of Genetics

Cambridge, UK

Hi Dominique,
Inside the script you are running you should see a part like this:
#! Which project should be charged:
#SBATCH -A bridge-hcm-sl2-cpu
If your script doesn't have an sl2 account here then this would be why your maximum job run time is less.

Chris

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Dominique,
Ah ok 36 hours would be the maximum run time that you would have at the moment. Would you be able to look at checkpointing your job so that you could split it into multiple jobs? Requesting multiple nodes may also help reduce the runtime you need. Otherwise, you may need to apply for access to the long queue.
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html)
Kind Regards
Chris
</MESSAGE>

Hi Chris, 
  
I just wanted to re-open this ticket, as I the issue I am having at the moment seems quite similar to our previous thread: 
  
I found a way to get around the memory issue by using sbatch array options (sbatch --array=733-8358 missense_revel_controls_var_count.peta4-icelake).  
  
This was working relatively ok; however, the number of jobs being taken from pending to running started to decline, and each job started to take much longer to complete (whereas it wasn't like this in the beginning). 
For example, squeue would show something like this:  
9234706_[1263-8358   icelake   cpujob hpcwest1 PD       0:00      1 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)    
9234706_1263         icelake   cpujob hpcwest1 R       40:13      1 cpu-q-400 
(with very little of these individual tasks actually being executed) 
  
I noticed that in my sbatch script, I had ntasks= 68. So I thought maybe taking the number of tasks back to the default ntasks = 1 might help quicken the speed as well as the number of scripts being run simultaneously (since the array option is already being implemented)…  
  
However, now I seem to have a new issue where the individual scripts are being killed, and this is the error message of one of the jobs: 
/var/spool/slurm/slurmd/job9234770/slurm_script: line 133: 2840038 Killed                  python3 fetching_vcf_controls_info2.2_revel_incorp.py 796 1           slurmstepd: error: Detected 1 oom-kill event(s) in StepId=9234770.batch cgroup. Some of your processes may have been killed by the cgroup out-of-memory handler. 
  
Do you have any suggestions as to how I can optimise this and avoid the scripts getting killed please? 
  
Kind Regards 
Dominique

[SUMMARY] Need of more time / power

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,   
I am trying to do an analysis which involves randomizing a 1000 x 1000 matrix 10000 times so I can compare the similarity of two of these matrices to a null distribution.  
It is a script in Matlab. Despite calling for an InteractiveJob with time of 120 hours and  -c30 -mem-per-cpu=8g and activating the "parallel pool" mode of matlab ,It approximately completes 1% every 24 h.  Thus supposedly completing in 3 months (By which time the interactive job closes). 
How would I go about getting more power and time to perform this analysis? 
Any advice would be much appreciated All the bestPeter Coppola  
PhD student University Division of Anaesthesia

Hi Jeffrey,   
Thank you for your prompt reply!  
restructuring the code may be a problem because one would have to create a null distribution with the entire matrix. A possible solution would maybe be to run say 500 randomizations in parallel 20 times and somehow put all the 20 sets of 500 correlations in one null distribution.   
The problem with this is that I use a toolbox developed for representational similarity analysis and I wouldn't know how to change it in the right way.  I am using a matrix comparison testing function for connectivity matrices which is not what it was designed for but i think could yield promising results and possibly a new way to analyse this type of data.   
If you can find a way to parallelise it that would be very helpful!   
attached are the relevant codes and data.  
Of particular interest is the function called  rsa.compareRefRDM2candRDMs  . To make it run you will also have to tell matlab where the userOptions function is.   
My script is called IMAGE_sc_scr.m; The matrices to be compared are the AV_CON_MAT and AV_MOD_MAT. (the latter one need to be in a call for the rsa.compareRefRDM2candRDMs function to work ). I don't think it loads well onto email, but I suppose you could create a random matrix.   
It looks like I cant send the mat files. You could create a random 1000x1000 matrix to test the script ?   
Looking forward to you reply  
In the mean time have a lovely reply 
Peter

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Peter, 
sorry for the delay in getting back to you. Nothing obvious occurs to me as to how we might speed this process up. Furthermore, it is not possible to allow 100 day long jobs. Even if we were to ignore the impact on other users of the system, we would not necessarily expect the system to stay up for 100 continuous days. 
However, a potential different solution to this problem would be to use an external checkpoint/restart tool DMTCP. Please see here [1] an example job script that makes use of DMTCP to checkpoint and restart a long running workload.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
[1] [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html#note-on-checkpointing](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html#note-on-checkpointing)
</MESSAGE>

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Chris, 
  
Okay, checkpointing seems like a good idea (one I had never heard of until now so thank you very much).  
  
I can't find much documentation on how to integrate this using slurm in the hpc.  
  
I have found that adding this: 
  
#SBATCH --checkpoint=360  # Time in minutes, every X minutes to make a checkpoint 
#SBATCH --checkpoint-dir=/data/ckpt # Default place where your checkpoints will be created 
  
To the beginning of my script might help? But I am unsure of what I would run if the script terminates at some stage and how it knows to pick up from the latest checkpoint?  
  
If you have any guidance or an example then I'd be so grateful! 
  
Kind Regards 
Dominique

From: Jeffrey Salmond <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: Tuesday, June 4, 2019 3:07 PM
To: P. Coppola
Subject: HPCSSUP-17507 Need of more time / power 
   
—-—-—-—
Reply above this line.
Jeffrey Salmond commented:
Hi Peter,
sorry for the delay in getting back to you. Nothing obvious occurs to me as to how we might speed this process up. Furthermore, it is not possible to allow 100 day long jobs. Even if we were to ignore the impact on other users of the system, we would not necessarily expect the system to stay up for 100 continuous days.
However, a potential different solution to this problem would be to use an external checkpoint/restart tool DMTCP. Please see here [1] an example job script that makes use of DMTCP to checkpoint and restart a long running workload.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.

user_input:
Dear Support
I started to copy a 108GB zip file to RCS when it occurred to me that this might be a bad idea. It quickly completed anyway. Do you recommend that I don't do this or is it OK?
Kind regards
Simon
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RCS Transfer Best Practice
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asks if transferring a 108GB zip file to RCS is problematic. Large archives are acceptable. For RCS workflows, tar is usually preferred; zip is still acceptable where password protection or Windows tooling requires it. Bigger single archives are typically slower to stage/retrieve, so archive layout should match expected access/retrieval patterns.

ACTION
1) Confirm transfer is acceptable.
2) Recommend best-practice docs.
3) Prefer tar where feasible; zip acceptable when required (e.g. password protection).
4) Explain operational trade-off of archive sizing (fewer large files vs faster partial retrieval).
5) Recommend integrity verification after transfer (e.g. checksum comparison).

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Simon,

That is generally fine. For RCS, tar archives are usually preferred, but zip is acceptable if needed (for example for password protection).

The main trade-off is that very large archives can take longer to retrieve from cold storage. If you expect partial restores, splitting data into sensible archive chunks can help operationally.

After transfer, it is also good practice to verify integrity (for example with checksums) between source and destination.

Best-practice guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/best-practice.html

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not present size threshold as a hard prohibition unless policy defines one.
- Do not overstate archive-size rules; frame them as performance/operational trade-offs.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / File Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is concerned about copying a 108GB zip file to RCS and wants to know if it's okay to do so [1]. According to the documentation, RCS works best with larger files, and it's recommended to create a small number of large files rather than many small ones [2][5]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To address the user's concern, we can inform them that copying a 108GB zip file to RCS is acceptable, but it's essential to be aware of the potential performance implications [2][5]. We can also suggest that they consider compressing the file or splitting it into smaller files if possible [2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your concern. Copying a 108GB zip file to RCS is acceptable, but please be aware that it may impact performance. We recommend creating a small number of large files rather than many small ones. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- It's crucial to be mindful of the file size and type when copying to RCS to avoid potential performance issues [2][5].
- Users should be aware of the RCS best practices and guidelines for storing and managing files [2][5].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-31203
[2] : HPCSSUP-45123
[5] : HPCSSUP-39109

[SUMMARY] RCS storage

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support, 
We are try to optimise our use of RCS storage. 
We are working with tar files, about 4GB in size, 10 files per experiment. The advice was that we should compress them again into one big archive. Is that the optimal solution or should we be transferring 10 files per folder, and skip the extra compression step. 
Many thanks, 
Radek 
 
 
Radoslaw Lach | Research Associate
Early Cancer Institute, University of Cambridge
Department of Oncology, Hutchison Research Centre
Box 197 Cambridge Biomedical Campus, Cambridge, CB2 0XZ
E: [rl381@cam.ac.uk](mailto:rl381@cam.ac.uk)T: 01223 376 602
[www.earlydetectioncambridge.org.uk/](http://www.earlydetectioncambridge.org.uk/)        
[FILE ATTACHMENT]

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Radek,
RCS works best with larger files, so if you can create a 40GB tar file rather than 10x 4GB files then that is preferable. If each individual tar is already compressed then you can probably skip another compression step and just simply make one large tar, so you can simply omit any compression flag you're passing to tar as tar doesn't compress by default.
Best,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Simon,
There have been discussions with the team running CenCom about allowing PIs to keep the links between permissions set within it, and storage on RDS. I'm not part of those discussions, so it might be worth raising it with them if you wanted to do that as well.
My understanding is that this would be useful if you had data from several WBIC projects located in a single RDS storage area, so that users who had been given access to the RDS storage would also need to be in the relevant WBIC groups to look at particular directories.
Alternatively we can 'reset' the permissions on all the data in your RDS area as you mention, by changing the group ownership to either the users or managers usergroup. The decision will need to be based on whether you need to enforce different permissions for the directories.
I'd expect data downloaded to be picking up the group of the containing directory. Is the guidance at [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html#pacs-access](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html#pacs-access) of any help?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0008 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Leon
I had written a long reply to this but the details are probably too complicated. Maybe Tim and James have an opinion? I have cc'd them but they are already attached to the thread.
Preserving the current wbic groups on the HPC data storage (not the WBIC DICOM server which is a separate issue) has attractions and I toyed with the idea of deliberately using them to restrict access in the HPC shared storage. But it requires that all users have permissions on CenCom updated, which isn't always happening. I might be able to add users through Access Control Lists if they aren’t in a group (user trumps group?) but this is not simplifying things and I can't do that for the parent directories holding the files are owned by root. This would only work if I started afresh copying everything to a new file structure.
Removing the wbic groups simplifies the sharing of data. And the new HPC Manager/User groups would enable us to impose some order. As long as the wbic groups still exist on the HPC we can still change any directory's group status to wbic-p00363 or whatever was appropriate on an ad hoc basis.
*Basically, do we want to keep the wbic protocol access restrictions as previously or do we want to remove some or all of them, granting read access to all users of the space by default. And then restrict access on an ad hoc basis.*
It also appears possible to additionally partition the RDS space into subspaces, although I am not sure how that works. The new space is listed as 36TB unallocated for me at [https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/) but clearly has been allocated.
In relation to my access to the DICOM server I appear to have permission to access files today (I was blocked from both the HPHI and HPC) so maybe some changes were made following the CenCom discussions you describe.
Regards,
Simon

My understanding is that this would be useful if you had data from several WBIC projects located in a single RDS storage area, so that users who had been given access to the RDS storage would also need to be in the relevant WBIC groups to look at particular directories.
Alternatively we can 'reset' the permissions on all the data in your RDS area as you mention, by changing the group ownership to either the users or managers usergroup. The decision will need to be based on whether you need to enforce different permissions for the directories.

----Original Message----
From: Simon Jones [spj24@cam.ac.uk](mailto:spj24@cam.ac.uk) 
Sent: 09 June 2021 10:16
To: CBU-HPC Pseudo-User
Cc: Timothy Rittman; James Rowe
Subject: RE: https://ucam-rcs.atlassian.net/browse/HPCSSUP-35780#icft=HPCSSUP-35780 Rowe Group RDS and RCS transfer from HPHI to HPC
Dear Leon
I had written a long reply to this but the details are probably too complicated. Maybe Tim and James have an opinion? I have cc'd them but they are already attached to the thread.
Preserving the current wbic groups on the HPC data storage (not the WBIC DICOM server which is a separate issue) has attractions and I toyed with the idea of deliberately using them to restrict access in the HPC shared storage. But it requires that all users have permissions on CenCom updated, which isn't always happening. I might be able to add users through Access Control Lists if they aren’t in a group (user trumps group?) but this is not simplifying things and I can't do that for the parent directories holding the files are owned by root. This would only work if I started afresh copying everything to a new file structure.
Removing the wbic groups simplifies the sharing of data. And the new HPC Manager/User groups would enable us to impose some order. As long as the wbic groups still exist on the HPC we can still change any directory's group status to wbic-p00363 or whatever was appropriate on an ad hoc basis.
*Basically, do we want to keep the wbic protocol access restrictions as previously or do we want to remove some or all of them, granting read access to all users of the space by default. And then restrict access on an ad hoc basis.*
It also appears possible to additionally partition the RDS space into subspaces, although I am not sure how that works. The new space is listed as 36TB unallocated for me at [https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/) but clearly has been allocated.
In relation to my access to the DICOM server I appear to have permission to access files today (I was blocked from both the HPHI and HPC) so maybe some changes were made following the CenCom discussions you describe.
Regards,
Simon

<MESSAGE id=0011 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Only people who have been added to the HPC system as Users or Managers of your HPC storage space can have access.
If we create new directories under the space they will either be in the 'User' or 'Manager' group. Or if individual users prefer they can change the group to their own CRSID to help restrict access to their own files, o I think they could change the group to any of the old HPHI protocol groups they like such as p00373, provided CenCom has made them a member of that group.
The HPHI files have been copied across preserving the old HPHI protocol groups. I can change any of the subdirectories that I own to be in any group. But I cannot change the top directory because it is owned by root. This seems to have been done deliberately to attempt to preserve the integrity of the HPHI group structure. They can be copied and then ownership of the copied files becomes mine. But I am not tyring to find a ways round a system - I am just trying to make something everybody is happy with.
Tim's proposal to have a flat directory structure devoid of protocols would need the group directories to be dismantled. Or we could maybe set up a flat system of symbolic links to a protocol structure.
We expect to add members outside the group to the Rowe space. For instance, Young Hong and Tim Fryer. If the HPHI protocol groups were preserved then we could have it so that they could only access PET protocols. If we remove the HPHI protocols then Young and Tim might have access to any files like any other user. They will also be able to access other users' files unless individuals choose to protect their own files by changing permissions. 
If we were very strict we could stop users creating files other than in a protocol archive or group directory. This would be a system more similar to that under the HPHI.
It is also possible to fine tune access through Access Control Lists but this could become unmanageable unless it was applied to a limited number of directories. A deployment of two groups, Mangers and Users together with an individual's choice to use their own CRSID group could provide easy sharing between members of the Rowe Group and invited people outside the group. But this is less default privacy by design.
As an aside, all files in your older Genfi Storage Space are owned by group members and are not currently in the HPHI protocol groups. I could always change them to be in any of the groups I am a member of. So, I could change the genfi files that I own to be in q10017, which is the genfi protocol, or I could put them in p00373 NIMROD, but I am not suggesting to do that.
There probably isn't a practical middle ground. We either choose an open system so that all group members can access most files, or the current stricter protocol system requiring all users to be correctly allocated to protocols in CenCom. In that case other users like Tiago who have not been added to an HPHI group, would have to be fed files by users who are in the group. We would then try to do away with the Manager and User groups or use them in parallel in some extra directories. 
I am still worried about creation of a chaotic set of directories and files. The old group share resulted in an intermingling of files with different owners. Users will come and go and you need something that can survive for the long term. I counted 406 files called 'core' that I could read of mixed ownership in just one group directory. These are from crashes and can be large files in some cases. I don't have permission to delete some of these files because the user has restricted delete access. What happens when these users leave the group.
We could discuss this after the meeting tomorrow and others might have different ideas?
Simon

<MESSAGE id=0014 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Simon,
While it's always possible for a determined user who has write access to a file/directory to take ownership of it and restrict access by others, the model for the new RDS projects does make that less likely to happen by accident. Read/write access by members of the managers group comes from an ACL rather than just user/group ownership, so changing ownership itself doesn't block managers from regaining control. If all those ACLs were removed, then a ticket to the servicedesk from a Data Manager could request those to be restored/changed.
From the outside, I would have assumed that a user that was deliberately removing access to project data by their PI and managers, would be more of an administrative/management problem, rather than technical. If there were valid reasons (in their view) to restrict access from outside their immediate group, that might be an argument to consider splitting the storage into several projects with different userlists.
The quota system doesn't allow for reporting on user or group usage within a project, just usage across the project itself. Drilling down into per-user usage would have to be done by walking the directory tree to calculate sizes.
Would you like us to amend permissions on the rds-k1PgZFWfeZY project now, or wait for further views? I would be thinking about;
1. Changing group ownership on all data to be rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers
2. Setting ACLs on all data to allow read/write access by managers and read-write access by users
3. Setting default ACLs on all directories to have read-write access by managers and users to newcly created files/directories.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0015 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Leon
Yes, as you say, if a user has write permission anywhere - and all users will have somewhere - then they can create a directory and make it read only to them. And they can then fill it with any amount of data and any number of files but we can't assess this. This was also the situation in the HPHI but there it was a problem for the HPHI (or WBIC) rather than us as an individual group. And the HPHI were able to report on an individual's usage. 
There are files currently on archive and group that I cannot access and so did not know the size of the data. This may have been done with good intentions. We will probably have to request that users not remove read access to Managers. If they need to preserve privacy they can use their personal RDS space or if this is not enough they need a separate paid storage.
1. and 2. I had thought to set the group on all files in /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/archive to be rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers and ACLs to allow read-write to rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers and read only to rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-users (preserving what was mostly there at present). And all files in /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/group to rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-users and allow read-write to rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers and rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-users. I am not sure there is a significant difference between making the group rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers or rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-users if the ACLs are also set for both. If you recommend setting them all to rds-k1PgZFWfeZY-managers that might just be easier.
It would have been nice if write permissions on all directories and files had remained as it was a present where a user has made a file read only to the group. But that is probably not practical so any individual owners of files will have to remove group write permission later if they have to protect them.
I envisage that there may be some files in /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/archive that a user wants to write to but we can deal with that later by maybe moving them to /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/group
3. Set default access to newly created files in /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/archive to read-write access by managers and read only to users. We can maybe add user write access if necessary to some directories in the future (I am thinking of Young Hong and the like). Set default access to newly created files in /rds/project/rds-k1PgZFWfeZY/group to read-write access by managers and users. Users will have to explicitly remove write permission, as they did in the HPHI.
Please correct me if this does not look sensible.
Regards,
Simon

[SUMMARY] Writing to cold storage

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support,
I’d like to back up my data to cold storage and am looking to use something like:
tar cvzf /rcs/project/cc234/rcs-cc234-pbc-backup/outfile.tar.gz ~/rds/rds-pbcp_collab-QbMvoEmrCLY/source_directory
One of the directories I’d like to compress consists of ~1,000 bam files, each ~= 200GB. My concern is that this would create a very large .tar.gz.
Do you have any advice on if this is ok when writing to the rcs, or is it better to split into smaller tar files? And also, how I can find out the rcs quota we currently have so that I don’t exceed it.
Many thanks!
Charlotte

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=OTHER>
Hi @Stuart Rankin , do you know thw point of contact for this? I think maybe Leon? 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi @Henry Carr - any storage questions you’re not sure about, just ping me or @Gwen Dawes on the ticket (or slack if it’s quicker)
For this one, Charlotte is fine - 200GB is ok and we prefer people to create big files on RCS as it makes it quicker to recover from tape. Having thousands of tiny files takes days to retrieve as each one has to be read individually from tape, and the tape has to be wound back and forwards to the right place for each one.
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=OTHER>
Thank you @Leon Bond - should I go ahead and assign this one to you?

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Yep, I’ll grab it now off you.
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Charlotte,
200GB is ok to save to RCS, generally we prefer people to create a small number of larger files rather than many small ones, as it is much faster to retrieve later from tape.
Unfortunately there isn't a way we can make the usage directly visible to you, it's something that we currently need to check within the internal systems that manage the RCS filesystem.
There is around 461TB of data in the rcs-cc234-pbc-backup project currently.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

benchmark_annotation.summary: Copying large file to RCS

retrieval_trace.text:
[TICKET_SUMMARY] Couldn't copy a large size file

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
I am writing because I couldn’t copy a large size file (~3GB) from the rcs to rds folder any longer. Could I have any advice regarding this issue?
Many thanks,
Taehoon

[TICKET_SUMMARY] RCS storage

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Radek,
RCS works best with larger files, so if you can create a 40GB tar file rather than 10x 4GB files then that is preferable. If each individual tar is already compressed then you can probably skip another compression step and just simply make one large tar, so you can simply omit any compression flag you're passing to tar as tar doesn't compress by default.
Best,
Dominic

</MESSAGE>

[TICKET_SUMMARY] slow transfer to RCS

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>

I've checked with colleagues on the RCS platform, we're not aware of a general issue and the general traffic in/out of the cluster doesn't appear too bad. Are you doing this via a login node or the SSH gateway ones?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

user_input:
We are having problems creating a GPU platform on the “ska-src-science-validations” tenancy on Azimuth (with OpenHPC v3).
I always seem to have the error:
error creating blazar lease - Not enough hosts available
and it doesn’t matter which flavour (standard or v2) to use for login and control nodes.
Does this mean that I’m doing something wrong in the setup, or is there an issue with blazar on that tenancy?
Thanks a lot,
Ed
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Cloud Capacity / Azimuth / Blazar Leasing
Technical Action Required: No (capacity explanation)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
`Not enough hosts available` in Blazar indicated resource exhaustion, not a user misconfiguration.

ACTION
1) Explain that message reflects current capacity saturation.
2) Advise retry when capacity frees.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Edward,

That error indicates capacity is currently exhausted in the tenancy; it is not necessarily a setup error on your side.

Please retry when resources become available.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest configuration changes if capacity is the real limiter.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Azimuth / GPU Platform Creation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The error message "error creating blazar lease - Not enough hosts available" suggests that there are not enough available hosts to create the GPU platform on Azimuth [2]. This issue has been encountered before, and it is likely due to the lack of free Blazar host capacity [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the current resource allocation and usage in the "ska-src-science-validations" tenancy to determine if there are any available hosts that can be used to create the GPU platform [2].
2. If there are no available hosts, consider terminating or reconfiguring existing platforms to free up resources [1].
3. Alternatively, consider using a different flavor or configuration for the login and control nodes to reduce the resource requirements [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are there any existing platforms or resources that can be terminated or reconfigured to free up hosts for the new GPU platform?
- Are there any specific requirements or constraints for the GPU platform that may be contributing to the resource availability issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with creating a GPU platform on Azimuth. We are investigating the current resource allocation and usage in your tenancy to determine the cause of the issue. If you have any existing platforms or resources that can be terminated or reconfigured to free up hosts, please let us know. We will work with you to find a solution to create the GPU platform.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When creating a new platform on Azimuth, ensure that there are sufficient available hosts to avoid resource conflicts [2].
- Be cautious when terminating or reconfiguring existing platforms to avoid disrupting ongoing work or workflows [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-82544
[2] : HPCSSUP-84078

[SUMMARY] Azimuth: `Error: not enough hosts`

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear support team,
Since Azimuth now uses Blazar for compute resource reservation, the 
unallocated compute resources for the ska-src-cambridge tenancy are now 
longer available. Attempts to create a computing platform on Azimuth 
result in the error `Error: Not enough hosts`.
Last week me, Martin Hardcastle, Bonny Barkus and Paul Browne had a few 
discussions on Slack (see links below) about how to move forward. 
Ideally, resources which are currently unallocated would be moved to the 
blazar system whilst the resources currently still in use will remain 
as-is until their jobs are complete or interruptible.
Relevant Slack links:
- [https://skao.slack.com/archives/C070216V71D/p1739460007545779](https://skao.slack.com/archives/C070216V71D/p1739460007545779)
- [https://skao.slack.com/archives/C070216V71D/p1741342008392039](https://skao.slack.com/archives/C070216V71D/p1741342008392039)
With this email I'd like to raise a support ticket to record the 
discussion above, and would like to ask if it is possible for 
unallocated compute resources to be moved to the blazar system.
Kind regards and thanks in advance!
– 
Matthijs van der Wild
/mɑˈtɛi̯s vɑn dər 'ʋɪɫt/

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=OTHER>
Dear Matthijs van der Wild , 
Thank you for your message. I will be looking into this for you and will respond as soon as I have some more information. 
In the meantime, should you have any additional updates, questions or comments, please reply and update this ticket. 
 
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=OTHER>
Hi @Paul Browne are you aware of any issues?
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Matthijs,
The issue is that there are really not very many unallocated resources sufficient to clear enough hosts to move into Blazar (Blazar can only accept fully empty hosts) or (more practically) shuffle the existing workload around into.
@Yuchen Liu , by my count you have 5 entirely separate Slurm compute appliances, along with the A100 GPU instance and an additional Slurm appliance slurm-sdc3 you could probably access in the EoR SDC3B project; is sitting on all of this compute resource really necessary?

##Existing Slurm appliances for yl871
yl871-slurm
yl871-slurm2
yl871-slurm3
yl871-slurm4
yl871-slurm-general-large
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_PRINCIPAL_INVESTIGATOR>
Hi Paul,
We are currently using the 5 Slurm appliances for parallelized simulations. However I expect by the end of the month, once the simulations are completed, we may be able to remove some of them or combine the small appliances into a general-large appliance.
Best,
Yuchen
</MESSAGE>

Hi Simon, if it's getting as far as trying some HPL synthetic benchmarks then probably the instances are all there. We can look in the logging why it's failing to run tests, but in the meantime time you could try get into the login instance for the cluster and see if the GPU resource is there.

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Simon, on this one, were you able to get into the Slurm cluster and check on the status of the GPU compute in there at all?

Many thanks,
Paul B.

</MESSAGE>

user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I use the university MS authenticator on my phone and I would like to 
set it up as my TOTP for HPC login
Is it possible and how do I do this?
I tried using the TOTP when I attempt to login but it does not seem to 
work.
Thank you!
Cheers, Claudio
– 
Claudio Castelnovo
Professor of Theoretical Physics and Fellow of Trinity College
/Theory of Condensed Matter Group/
Department of Physics, Cavendish Laboratory
University of Cambridge
19, J J Thomson Avenue, Cambridge CB3 0HE, UK
phone: +44 (0)1223 337433
fax: +44 (0)1223 337356
web: [http://www.tcm.phy.cam.ac.uk/profiles/cc726/](http://www.tcm.phy.cam.ac.uk/profiles/cc726/)
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Provisioning / MFA / New User
Technical Action Required: No (process guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asks whether the Microsoft Authenticator app on their phone can be used for HPC login TOTP and reports that the current code does not work.

The same authenticator app can be used, but CSD3 requires its own separate MFA enrolment/token; a University token will not work for CSD3 SSH or web login. If the user does not yet have an active CSD3 account, account provisioning must be completed before MFA enrolment can work.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user already has an active CSD3 account.
2) If not, direct them to the application form:
   https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application
3) Once the account is provisioned, instruct them to enrol CSD3 MFA via the documented process, e.g. SSH to:
   `ssh cc726@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the same Microsoft Authenticator app can be used, but it must contain a separate CSD3 token.
5) Do NOT:
   - Tell the user to reuse an existing University/UIS token for CSD3.
   - Troubleshoot login-web credentials before account provisioning is confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Claudio,

Yes, you can use the same Microsoft Authenticator app on your phone for HPC login, but CSD3 needs its own separate token. The existing University token will not work for CSD3.

Before troubleshooting the token further, we first need to confirm whether you already have an active CSD3 account. If not, please apply here:

https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application

Once your account is active, please follow the MFA setup guide and connect to:

  ssh cc726@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

to enrol the CSD3 MFA token in your authenticator app.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish account provisioning from MFA setup.
- The app can be reused; the token cannot.
- Do not assume the user can log in via web/SSH before the account exists.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / TOTP Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to set up Microsoft Authenticator as their TOTP for HPC login, but it's not working [4]. The TOTP for CSD3 is separate from the University of Cambridge one, and the user needs to set up a new TOTP specifically for HPC [4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, we need to reset the user's TOTP token and guide them through setting up a new TOTP for HPC. We will schedule a video call to verify the user's identity and then reset their TOTP token [2][3][6]. After the token is reset, the user can set up a new TOTP using the instructions provided on the HPC wiki [2][4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are you available for a video call to verify your identity and reset your TOTP token?
- Have you tried setting up a new TOTP using the instructions on the HPC wiki [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To set up Microsoft Authenticator as your TOTP for HPC login, we need to reset your current TOTP token and guide you through setting up a new one. We will schedule a video call to verify your identity and then reset your token. Please let us know your availability for the call.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The TOTP for CSD3 is separate from the University of Cambridge one, and users need to set up a new TOTP specifically for HPC [4].
- We need to verify the user's identity before resetting their TOTP token [2][3][6].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : HPCSSUP-50253
[3] : HPCSSUP-94445
[4] : HPCSSUP-51576
[6] : HPCSSUP-60760

user_input:
Hello,
I hope this email finds you well!
I am working on CSD3 for some cpu jobs, particularly nodes cclake and Icelake. I submitted some jobs last week and I found the queueing time very long. I am wondering if you can give advice on this regarding if there is anything I did wrong causing the long wait. Some general info:
- Name: Hongyu Zhou
- Account/PI: N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
- Working directory: /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report
- Task:
- Data: I work with bibliographic data on hundreds of millions of paper including their title, abstract, and citation relationships
- Task:
- I am trying to calculate the cooccurence frequency of concepts in papers (two concepts used in one paper), for which the concepts are extracted already in our current data (one paper associated with a list of paper).
- I am building these cooccurrence networks for using papers since 1990 to current and group them to a interval of 3 years
- I generate networks for every three years.
- Problem:
- The queueing time is quite long.
- Slurm Job_id=10784619 Name=build_cooccur_net Began, Queued time 17:19:49
- I tried to minimise the potential RAM usage by using smaller intervals, using smaller samples and use sql-like ducked to calculate the concurrences. But in the future, there will definitely be larger jobs I need to run considering the size of my data in nature, especially if we were use Node2Vec to embed the networks we obtained.
- Job submission script:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J build_cooccur_net
#SBATCH -A N-LAWRENCE-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=6
#SBATCH --mem=30G
#SBATCH --time=4:00:00
#SBATCH --output=1_build_net_duck_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=1_build_net_duck_%j.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=hz488@cam.ac.uk
set -euo pipefail
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job started"
module purge
module load python/3.8
source "$HOME/hzenv/bin/activate"
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
export OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1
TEMP_DIR="${SLURM_TMPDIR:-./temp_edges}"
mkdir -p "$TEMP_DIR"
python /home/hz488/project/ai_ranking_report/scripts/1_build_net_save_duckdb.py \
--input-dir /home/hz488/project/dimensions/publications/ \
--output-dir /home/hz488/project/data/ai_ranking_report/data/1_build_net \
--batch-size 200000 \
--batches-per-file 50 \
--sample-frac 1
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %T')] Job finished”
Could you kindly advise me on this?
Best,
Hongyu
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Scheduling / Queue Wait Times / Service Levels
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on non-paying SL3 CPU saw long waits. Correct explanation: paid workloads have higher priority; queue times fluctuate; cancelling/resubmitting loses accrued priority.

ACTION
1) Reassure no job/account fault detected.
2) Explain SL3 prioritisation and variable wait times.
3) Advise against cancel/resubmit behavior.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Hongyu,

From the information provided, there is no obvious sign of a problem with your account or submission. Your jobs are on the non-paying SL3 service level, and paid activity is prioritised ahead of it.

Queue time can vary significantly. Please avoid cancelling/resubmitting, as that can lose accrued priority.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Avoid promising specific start-time estimates on shared queues.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Queue Time
Technical Action Required: No
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing long queue times for their jobs on the CSD3 cluster, specifically on the icelake partition [1][2]. The user's job submission script requests 1 node, 6 CPUs per task, and 30G of memory, with a time limit of 4 hours [1]. The queue time for the job is 17 hours and 19 minutes [1]. Similar issues have been reported by other users, with long queue times due to high utilization of the cluster [3][4][5].

ACTION
The user can try to optimize their job submission script to reduce the queue time. This can be done by requesting fewer resources, such as fewer nodes or CPUs, or by using a different partition [2][6]. The user can also try to submit their job at a less busy time, such as during the night or on weekends [7]. Additionally, the user can consider using the sapphire partition, which appears to be less congested [6].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about your job submission script and the resources you are requesting?
- Have you tried submitting your job to a different partition or at a less busy time?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your long queue times on the CSD3 cluster. We understand that this can be frustrating, especially when you have time-sensitive jobs to run. We recommend trying to optimize your job submission script to reduce the queue time. You can try requesting fewer resources, such as fewer nodes or CPUs, or using a different partition. Additionally, you can try submitting your job at a less busy time, such as during the night or on weekends. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out to us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should be aware that long queue times are expected during periods of high utilization [3][4][5].
- The user should not cancel and resubmit their job, as this can lead to a lower priority in the queue [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-88272
[2] : HPCSSUP-66025
[3] : HPCSSUP-47777
[4] : HPCSSUP-68929
[5] : HPCSSUP-89152
[6] : HPCSSUP-67396
[7] : HPCSSUP-16387

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-88272
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2025-05-23T12:32:18.924+0100
[SUMMARY] Question regarding HPC Queueing time

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello!
I hope you are doing well! I just had a quick question that I was hoping you could help me with. Both yesterday and today I’ve noticed that my jobs are queuing for significantly longer than they normally do, despite not being time or memory intensive. The jobs I’m submitting are requesting 10,000mb of memory and 1hr run time, and yet will queue for extremely long periods of time. The fastest I’ve gotten jobs to run is after queueing for 30min, others I’ve had sitting in the queue for several hours and they still will not run. I’ve tried switching slurm partitions, including icelake, icelake-himem, cclake, and cclake-himem, and this does not seem to make a difference.
Do you have any advice on how I can fix this?
Thank you!
Best,
Amanda
–
Amanda Gardiner
PhD Student, Department of Genetics, University of Cambridge
MCR President, Christ’s College, University of Cambridge
ag2427@cam.ac.uk<[ag2427@cam.ac.uk](mailto:ag2427@cam.ac.uk)>
president@christsmcr.co.uk<[president@christsmcr.co.uk](mailto:president@christsmcr.co.uk)>

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Amanda,
There is nothing wrong with your account or jobs, except that there is a lot of jobs in the queue due to the time of year and staggered from the full maintenance three weeks ago.
Please bear in mind that each paying job can occupy its resources for 36 hours. Please be patient and don’t be tempted to cancel and resubmit - since all jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, that would be counterproductive. 
Your jobs will run when resources become available.
Best regards,
Elisabeth Reeve
HPC Support
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

Dear Support Desk,  
I'm so sorry to bother you with this question. My name is Christopher Bambic and I'm an M.Phil student at the Institute of Astronomy working with Professor Chris Reynolds through  his allocation REYNOLDS-SL3-CPU.   
I recently set up my account on CSD3 (username: cb979) in order to run the the simulations I've been working on at production scale, and I've noticed unexpectedly long queue times.  
I currently have requested 256 cores for 10 hours and my queued job has ID 10078604. While I understand that this is a large job request, I have been queued for well over 24 hours waiting for this job to run. Since I'm still new to CSD3, I'm not sure if this is an expected queue time or if I'm being queued for longer due to some reason I'm unaware of. I'm reaching out to you today on the suggestion of a number of postdoctoral researchers who said I should not be experiencing these sorts of queue times.  
Thus, I have the following questions:  
1) What sort of queue times are expected for jobs of ~200-500 cores (8-16 nodes)? 
2) Have I done anything which has caused me to have lower priority in the queue? 
3) If I have done something incorrectly which has lowered my priority, is there a way to earn higher priority?  
Thank you for your time and again I'm so sorry to bother you with this question. Since I'm only in Cambridge for one year, I'm getting concerned that I will be unable to finish my project(s) if I'm consistently queued for too long on each of my simulations.

Christopher Bambic '19

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Christopher,
Thanks for getting in-touch.
Unfortunately the wait time you are experiencing is currently typical for our service due to high utilisation. Over the past month and a half we've seen nearly 90% utilisation, which is unprecedented.
Your request is definitely on the large side, most of our user base are interested in high throughput, so often just request the full usage of a single node. Your job will be waiting on two things; building enough priority and waiting for your allocated nodes to clear.
You can get more information from the scheduler about which nodes you've been allocated and an estimated start time by using:
squeue -u cb979 --start
These values will change depending on the state of the queue. Due to the tiered service levels which we run (SL1, SL2, SL3) submissions from SL1 and SL2 accounts (paying users) will start with a higher priority and their jobs may begin before yours, that said, the longer your job is in the queue the more priority it'll acquire, so don't be tempted to cancel the job as this is counter productive.
The simplest way to get shorter queue times would be to negotiate some paid resources in the from of SL2 with your department. Our prices are available here: [https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies/charges](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/policies/charges)
If this is not possible, you may want to tuning around the --tasks-per-node to request fractions of a node. Obviously it'd request more nodes overall, but it would be able to utilise nodes which are in a 'mixed' state where not all their resources are being used. I'm not sure how effective this would be, but it might be worth a shot.
In general, it's a bit rough on the cluster for SL3 users at the moment.
Kind regards,
Dominic

Chris

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Question regarding HPC Queueing time

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello!
I hope you are doing well! I just had a quick question that I was hoping you could help me with. Both yesterday and today I’ve noticed that my jobs are queuing for significantly longer than they normally do, despite not being time or memory intensive. The jobs I’m submitting are requesting 10,000mb of memory and 1hr run time, and yet will queue for extremely long periods of time. The fastest I’ve gotten jobs to run is after queueing for 30min, others I’ve had sitting in the queue for several hours and they still will not run. I’ve tried switching slurm partitions, including icelake, icelake-himem, cclake, and cclake-himem, and this does not seem to make a difference.
Do you have any advice on how I can fix this?
Thank you!
Best,
Amanda
–
Amanda Gardiner
PhD Student, Department of Genetics, University of Cambridge
MCR President, Christ’s College, University of Cambridge
ag2427@cam.ac.uk<[ag2427@cam.ac.uk](mailto:ag2427@cam.ac.uk)>
president@christsmcr.co.uk<[president@christsmcr.co.uk](mailto:president@christsmcr.co.uk)>
